source.text:
Ava Chen: Quick idea — try a condensed 30m triage window for next two weeks to clear our >48h backlog. Proposal: 10m hotfix lane, 15m quick PR unblock, 5m owner assignments. Thoughts? :thinking_face:

Jamal Rivera: Seems doable. Can we shift from 11:00->11:30 to 10:30->11:00 to catch EU overlap?

Priya Nair: I prefer 10:30 start. Also can we add a recurring 'owner check' item to ensure tickets don't get stuck for 3+ days?

Luca Bianchi: +1 on owner check. Suggest we rotate the 'lead' each day — lead does the quick assignment and follow-ups.

Ava Chen: ok. Draft agenda:
```
10:30-10:40 hotfix lane (triage-bot flags)
10:40-10:55 PR/bug quick triage (assign owners)
10:55-11:00 owner rollcall + blockers
```

Marta Gómez: small ask — can we surface flaky tests and infra alerts separately? Maybe add a pinned thread in #eng-infra for infra FYIs.

Jamal Rivera: I'll take lead Mon/Wed this week. :thumbsup:

triage-bot: Reminder created — Mon/Wed 10:30-11:00 in #eng. Calendar invite sent to participants.

Priya Nair: Ack. I've updated the sprint board, added a 'triage-ready' label for tickets to surface in the agenda.

Luca Bianchi: One more — can we try a 2-week pilot then revisit metrics: avg time in backlog, #ownerless tickets, closed per session?

Ava Chen: Yep — we'll measure: avg time >48h, tickets closed per session, owner assignment rate. If no objections by EOD I'll update the cadence and ping ops to adjust calendar invites.

(Reaction notes: several :thumbsup: and a few :eyes: on the agenda.)

source.content:
Dylan Brooks: heads up — we’re ready to start the feature-flag rollout for Console > Optimize > Suggestions view (beta). Want to align on (1) internal dogfood plan, (2) who gets enabled first, (3) rollback criteria.

Marissa Cole: :thumbsup: yes. also want to make sure we’re consistent on language: “beta”, “estimated savings”, no guarantees.

Chloe Martin: backend is in good shape on staging. prod endpoint is behind the same flag + RBAC check. we’ll need an explicit allowlist per org.

Sean Gallagher: +1. SRE ask: staged rollout, measure p95 + error budget before enabling any external customer orgs.

Nora Ahmed: UI is behind `console_optimize_suggestions_beta` on web, but it also depends on API returning non-empty. empty states are working.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: RBAC note: users also need `optimize:suggestions:read`. otherwise they’ll get the “no access” empty state. enabling flag alone won’t help if the org’s roles aren’t set.

Marissa Cole: can we outline the exact sequence for dogfood? (flag + permissions + telemetry readiness)

Dylan Brooks: proposed sequence:
1) enable for Redwood internal org only (dogfood)
2) expand to a couple internal “friendly” orgs (Solutions + CS sandbox orgs)
3) enable for 2 external beta accounts (Northstar Support + Arkadia Finance) once telemetry checks pass

Logan Wright: telemetry readiness checklist for “Suggestions view not empty”:
- at least ~48h of request telemetry for the route/model pair
- token cost attribution pipeline not delayed (lag < 2h)
- cache metrics present if we want caching suggestions (hit rate, prefix cache enabled/observed)
If any missing, UI will show “insufficient telemetry” (expected).

Miguel Santos: for external, CS preference is we start with Northstar Support first. they’re already asking for cost controls and are more tolerant of beta UX.

Dev Patel: support readiness note: please don’t enable Arkadia without a quick internal FAQ / macro. we already had confusion on “risk level” wording with a different feature.

Tessa Morgan: design is final on the badge/copy. but agree: we should do internal dogfood for a few days to see what questions come up.

Sean Gallagher: SRE rollout guardrails:
- start with <5 orgs
- watch dashboards: API p95, 5xx rate, compute lag, and UI funnel drop-off
- rollback if: p95 > 500ms sustained for 15m or 5xx > 1% (or any tenant leakage, obviously)

Chloe Martin: API p95 is currently ~180ms in prod with flag OFF (baseline). when flag ON, we’ll see list queries + join. we have caching, but i’d like to watch it.

Dylan Brooks: what’s the mechanism for enabling per org? LaunchDarkly vs internal flags service?

Kaitlyn Nguyen: it’s LaunchDarkly for the console flag, and server-side org allowlist in Optimize service. both need to be toggled.

Dylan Brooks: ok so 2 toggles:
- LD flag: `console_optimize_suggestions_beta`
- Optimize allowlist: `optimize_suggestions_beta_org_allowlist`
Plus RBAC: grant `optimize:suggestions:read` to the org’s “Admin” role (or equivalent).

Marissa Cole: can we centralize this in a runbook-ish checklist? not asking you to write the doc here, but i want to avoid “it’s on for web but not API” confusion.

Sean Gallagher: +1. also log/audit requirements — when we enable a customer org, we should have an audit note.

Chloe Martin: we can use the admin tool to update allowlist with a reason string. example:
```
rwctl optimize suggestions allowlist add --org_id org_7Vh3... --reason "Suggestions beta: dogfood" --expires "2026-03-31"
```
(we can include TTL so we don’t forget it’s enabled)

Nora Ahmed: FYI console flag check is per org, not per user. but RBAC is per user so we’ll get “enabled but empty” tickets if roles aren’t right.

Marissa Cole: who owns enabling + verification for each stage?

Dylan Brooks: my suggestion:
- Internal dogfood enablement: Dylan + Nora
- Backend allowlist + sanity checks: Chloe
- RBAC roles: Kaitlyn
- SRE signoff for external customer enablement: Sean
- CS comms + expectation setting with customers: Miguel

Miguel Santos: works. for Northstar Support, i can schedule a 30-min check-in once it’s live. they’ll want to know “how do i apply this safely?”

Dev Patel: please make sure the UI has the “estimates are directional” disclaimer and a link to docs. reduces support churn.

Tessa Morgan: it does. also the risk tooltip clarifies it’s perf/reliability risk (not security). we saw that confusion in research.

Logan Wright: one more thing: if we enable too early while compute job hasn’t run yet, they’ll see “no suggestions yet.” not broken but confusing.

Chloe Martin: compute job cadence is hourly right now. first run after enable is within ~60m (worst case) unless lagged.

Sean Gallagher: can we gate external enables on “compute lag green” for 24h? i don’t want to page for this.

Dylan Brooks: yes. we’ll do internal enable today, let it run overnight, then decide external tomorrow.

Marissa Cole: who are the internal dogfooders? i’d like a short list so we actually get feedback.

Dylan Brooks: internal cohort:
- Console & Tooling (Nora, Dylan)
- Optimize PM (Marissa)
- Optimize PM (Aditya, if he wants)
- Applied ML (Maya / Jonas) — at least 1 reviewer
- SRE (Sean) for dashboard sanity

Kaitlyn Nguyen: FYI i can grant `optimize:suggestions:read` to `RedwoodEmployee` role in our internal org, so it “just works” for most folks.

Marissa Cole: perfect. please do that for internal only.

Sean Gallagher: external orgs should NOT inherit broad roles. keep it tight: Admins + the named beta contacts.

Miguel Santos: for Northstar, beta contacts are: (redacted here) but i’ll DM list. for Arkadia, they’re stricter; we should do Northstar first.

Dylan Brooks: timing proposal:
- Today 3pm PT: enable internal org + RBAC, validate UI + API + events
- Tomorrow 10am PT: review metrics, then enable Northstar
- 48h later: evaluate feedback + stability, then consider Arkadia

Logan Wright: event taxonomy reminder: impressions/expands/dismiss/apply-click. please confirm events are flowing after internal enable.

Nora Ahmed: will do. we log `suggestions_view_impression` once per page load and `suggestion_details_open` for drawer open. i’ll verify in the funnel dashboard.

ReleaseBot: Deployment scheduled: console-web @ 2026-02-22 15:15 PT (commit `8f1c2e1`) includes Suggestions page behind feature flag.

Chloe Martin: backend already deployed. only toggles remain.

Sean Gallagher: add rollback plan here so it’s explicit:
- if issues: turn OFF LD flag first (instant hides UI)
- if API is hot: remove org from allowlist (server denies, reduces load)
- if RBAC misconfig: revert role change and document.

Dylan Brooks: :thumbsup: sounds good. action items:
- Kaitlyn: grant internal org role permission `optimize:suggestions:read`
- Chloe: add internal org to allowlist w/ TTL; share rwctl command output in thread
- Nora: enable LD flag for internal org; validate UI states and emit events
- Logan/Sean: watch dashboards for 2h post-enable
- Miguel/Dev: prep customer enablement note + support macro draft (beta expectations)

Marissa Cole: thanks. please keep this thread updated with when each step is done + any weirdness.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: done. internal org role updated. audit note: “Suggestions beta dogfood enablement”

Chloe Martin: internal org added to allowlist (expires 2026-03-31). compute job should pick up within the hour.

Nora Ahmed: LD flag enabled for internal org. i can see the nav entry + Suggestions table (currently 3 rows for /v1/chat on llama-3.1-70b). empty states also look correct when switching projects.

Sean Gallagher: watching. API p95 stable so far (~210ms). no spike in 5xx.

Logan Wright: events showing up in the funnel dashboard. impression -> expand working. :thumbsup:

Dev Patel: please make sure we don’t enable external until docs link is live. otherwise we’ll get “what is this?” tickets.

Marissa Cole: docs link in UI points to the draft internal docs right now — we should swap to the public-ish docs URL before external.

Dylan Brooks: noted. we’ll block external enable until that link is updated + verified.

Miguel Santos: works for me. Northstar call is tentatively tomorrow afternoon; i’ll confirm once we’re green.

source.thread:
Ethan Park: anyone else seeing private upgrade rollbacks looping? getting reports of "stuck rollback" in installer logs
Priya Natarajan: yep, saw it in eu-west lab. looks like rollback job keeps re-queueing after it marks itself failed
Diego Fernandez: paste logs?
Ethan Park: 
```
installer-controller: rollback phase=apply prev_revision=42 status=FAILED
installer-controller: scheduling rollback retry backoff=30s
installer-controller: rollback phase=apply prev_revision=42 status=FAILED
... repeats ...
```
Ava Chen: CS is getting pinged by Acme, says upgrade has been "rolling back for hours"
Priya Natarajan: might be the cleanup hook never clearing the lock. feels like RRB-17 we had in staging
Diego Fernandez: workaround: delete the rollback lock CM + restart controller?
Priya Natarajan: yeah, in kube: `kubectl -n redwood-private delete cm installer-rollback-lock` then `rollout restart deploy/installer-controller`
Ethan Park: ack. can someone add a snippet to the upgrade runbook? also need an INT ticket for tracking
Incident Bot: Reminder: if customer-impacting, open #incidents thread + INT ticket.

source.content:
Cole Summers: Heads up — found a billing dimension mismatch in marketplace metering (AWS + GCP) in staging. Our internal meter IDs don’t line up with the new marketplace SKU dimensions.

Cole Summers: Symptoms: metering publish returns 4xx for unknown dimension on a subset of events.

Cole Summers: Example from billing-publisher logs:
```text
POST /marketplace/aws/metering
status=400
err="InvalidDimension" dimension="RW_HOSTED_TOKENS_TOTAL" request_id="req_8c1d..."
expected_dimensions=["RW_HOSTED_INPUT_TOKENS","RW_HOSTED_OUTPUT_TOKENS","RW_EMBEDDING_UNITS","RW_RERANK_UNITS"]
```

Nadia Rahman: :eyes: is this only AWS or GCP too?

Cole Summers: Both, but different failure modes.
- AWS: we’re sending TOTAL tokens dimension (legacy) but offer is configured with split in/out tokens.
- GCP: we’re *configured* for split in/out, but the billing catalog still maps hosted usage to the AWS-style TOTAL dimension.

Ethan Park: Where is the mapping coming from? billing-catalog config or marketplace adapter?

Cole Summers: Looks like billing-catalog. In `billing_catalog_marketplace_dimensions` we still have:
- `hosted.tokens.total -> RW_HOSTED_TOKENS_TOTAL`
But new SKU catalog v2 expects:
- `hosted.tokens.input -> RW_HOSTED_INPUT_TOKENS`
- `hosted.tokens.output -> RW_HOSTED_OUTPUT_TOKENS`

Chloe Martin: That explains why console onboarding “first call” succeeds but metering gets dropped later. Users won’t see it, but Finance will. :grimacing:

Priya Natarajan: Severity? Does this block submission / launch?

Cole Summers: Yes for launch. Partner portals validate dimensions in test transactions. If we meter against non-existent dimensions, the listing gets bounced.

Chris Osei: This is the issue that popped up in yesterday’s dry run, right? (we saw “metered usage pending” but nothing reconciled)

Cole Summers: Yup. And it maps to the support ticket SUP-4829 (dimension mismatch) we were hoping was just docs.

Markus Klein: Also surfaced with the prospect test flow last week — they asked why their AWS account showed $0 usage while Redwood Console showed tokens consumed.

Nadia Rahman: We need a quick fix + a backwards-compatible plan. Are there any existing marketplace customers already metered on TOTAL?

Cole Summers: On prod marketplace: small number of legacy subscriptions still on old listing (TOTAL tokens). We *cannot* break reconciliation for them.

Priya Natarajan: So we need dual-path based on offer/SKU version.

Cole Summers: Exactly.

Cole Summers: Proposed quick fix (today):
1) Update billing-catalog mapping to support both dimension sets.
2) Route by `marketplace_offer_version` (or SKU family) when emitting metering events.
3) Add temporary alias in metering pipeline: if dimension=RW_HOSTED_TOKENS_TOTAL and offer expects split, translate into input/output using our request breakdown (we already compute it for cost analytics).

Nadia Rahman: Do we have reliable input/output token counts at emit time? (depends where we meter)

Cole Summers: For Hosted requests: yes — usage record already includes `prompt_tokens` + `completion_tokens`. For any records missing breakdown (older SDK paths), we can fall back to TOTAL and only apply alias for offers that accept TOTAL.

Ethan Park: Where do we store `marketplace_offer_version` today?

Cole Summers: In entitlement payload (from sync job) we have `plan_external_id` + `marketplace_sku`. We can infer:
- `ri-hosted-v2-*` => split dimensions
- `ri-hosted-legacy-*` => total
I’d prefer a first-class flag but inference is fine for hotfix.

Chloe Martin: Can Console onboarding surface a warning if entitlement is v2 but metering is failing? Or at least emit telemetry.

Nadia Rahman: I can add telemetry on `metering_publish_failed` already. We’ll alert on spikes.

BillingBot: Alert: `marketplace-metering-publisher` has exceeded error budget in staging (5m). Top error: InvalidDimension.

Priya Natarajan: Timeline / owners?

Cole Summers: I’ll cut a PR in `redwood` today:
- Update billing catalog dimension map
- Add routing logic in marketplace metering emitter
- Add unit tests for AWS + GCP dimension sets
ETA: PR up in ~2 hours, can ship behind a flag.

Nadia Rahman: I’ll handle dashboards + alert threshold changes (we should page only on sustained failures, not transient AWS errors).

Ethan Park: I’ll sanity check entitlement payload fields + make sure sync job is populating `marketplace_sku` consistently (AWS and GCP).

Chris Osei: I’ll ping Partnerships/PMM that we found a blocker and we’re patching today. Also update the partner review tracker entry.

Markus Klein: Please tell me what the external behavior is if metering fails (do we hard-stop usage?). Prospects asked.

Cole Summers: No hard-stop. Usage continues. Metering failures queue + retry; but if dimension invalid, it’s a permanent failure until fixed. So we need to backfill once mapping is corrected.

Priya Natarajan: Backfill plan?

Cole Summers: After deploy:
- replay failed metering events from DLQ for last 7 days in staging
- for prod, replay from DLQ since listing refresh cutover date (we have request_ids)
We’ll keep idempotency keys so we don’t double-bill.

Nadia Rahman: Make sure idempotency key includes dimension name, otherwise translated events could collide.

Cole Summers: Good catch. I’ll set idempotency key to `subscription_id + usage_record_id + dimension`.

Ethan Park: One more thing: GCP dimensions naming has stricter limits (lowercase / underscores). Are we consistent?

Cole Summers: For GCP we’re using their dimension IDs (not display names). The mismatch is internal meter -> external dimension mapping, not the ID format. But I’ll verify.

Chloe Martin: Can we add a “metering status” debug panel for marketplace orgs? Support keeps asking how to tell if metering is flowing.

Priya Natarajan: Not for hotfix, but put a ticket in ENG backlog. We need it for ongoing.

Cole Summers: Agree. I’ll file a follow-up for:
- removing inference-by-sku in favor of explicit offer version
- better operator visibility (console + support tooling)

Chris Osei: For the immediate unblock, we should also update the marketplace submission docs/screenshots if they mention TOTAL tokens.

Nadia Rahman: Yeah, docs PR likely references TOTAL in a couple places. I’ll flag to @Alex Martinez / @Elliot Price in #docs once we confirm final dimension names.

Cole Summers: Final dimension names we should standardize on (v2):
- `RW_HOSTED_INPUT_TOKENS`
- `RW_HOSTED_OUTPUT_TOKENS`
- `RW_EMBEDDING_UNITS`
- `RW_RERANK_UNITS`
Legacy (keep for old listing only):
- `RW_HOSTED_TOKENS_TOTAL`

Priya Natarajan: OK. Ship hotfix today. Blocker until confirmed green in staging test purchase.

Cole Summers: Will post PR link here when up + tag for review.

Cole Summers: PR up: `pr-19879-marketplace-dimension-routing-hotfix` (adds dual mapping + translation). Need reviews from @Ethan Park + @Nadia Rahman.

Ethan Park: Looking now.

Nadia Rahman: Reviewing. Also adding metric `marketplace_metering_dimension_translated_count` so we know how often we’re doing the alias path.

Chloe Martin: Once merged, I’ll rerun the console marketplace E2E flow in staging (subscribe -> provision -> first call -> wait for metering tick).

Cole Summers: Merged + deployed to staging behind flag `marketplace_metering_dimension_routing_v2=true`.

BillingBot: Deploy complete: `marketplace-metering-publisher` staging @ 2026.02.?? build `a1f7c9d`.

Chloe Martin: Re-test results: metering calls now 200 OK. Seeing both dimensions emitted:
```text
metered dimension=RW_HOSTED_INPUT_TOKENS qty=12034
metered dimension=RW_HOSTED_OUTPUT_TOKENS qty=3098
```

Nadia Rahman: Dashboard is green. No InvalidDimension in last 30m.

Priya Natarajan: Good. Proceed to prod rollout after we confirm DLQ replay script.

Cole Summers: DLQ replay ran in staging successfully (no dupes). Will schedule prod replay + rollout with SRE window.

Chris Osei: Thanks all — I’m updating the partner review tracker + telling Partnerships we’re unblocked pending prod rollout/backfill.

source.thread:
Priya Natarajan: Heads up eng-wide: we’re kicking off the Standard Cost Model Spec + API initiative. Goal is to stop having 3 slightly different “cost” definitions (telemetry vs billing hooks vs Optimize prototypes) and converge on one canonical, versioned cost model that Console + Optimize + Finance can rely on.

Priya Natarajan: TL;DR
- We’re standardizing *inputs + semantics* (prefill vs decode, cache hits, batch efficiency, quantization deltas, overhead allocation) and exposing them via an internal Cost Model API.
- Console cost breakdowns and Optimize ROI/what-if will call the same API, not re-implement.
- This is *not* replacing Billing/RevRec systems. It’s a shared primitive layer + API.

Priya Natarajan: Why now
- We keep getting drift tickets (Console breakdown != invoice, what-if savings too optimistic).
- We can’t keep debating what “counts” per team; we need a spec + governance.

Priya Natarajan: Owners / leads
- Program: Talia Benson
- Service/API: Ethan Park (Platform Control Plane)
- Telemetry inputs: Logan Wright (Telemetry & Analytics)
- Console adoption: Chloe Martin (Console)
- Optimize adoption: Aditya Rao (Optimize)
- SRE/SLO: Rafael Mendes
- Finance alignment: Laura Bennett + Rishi Malhotra

Priya Natarajan: Milestones (Q1)
1) Discovery + requirements (2w): inventory existing cost defs + confirm v1 Console/Optimize use-cases
2) Spec + ADRs (2–3w): schema + semantics + compatibility/deprecation rules
3) Implementation (3–4w): cost-model service + client lib + conformance tests
4) Integration + rollout (2–3w): Console + Optimize behind flags + drift validation
5) Launch + governance: ongoing (review process, deprecation policy, docs)

Priya Natarajan: What to expect / migration
- If you have code computing “token cost” / “gpu cost” / “overhead” in product services, assume it’ll be deprecated.
- Console + Optimize will migrate first; other internal consumers should plan to follow once v1 stabilizes.
- Versioning: schema is explicit + versioned; clients must tolerate unknown fields (forward compat) and we’ll use planned deprecation windows.
- Bounded responses: API must handle high-cardinality telemetry without melting. Expect pagination/aggregation limits.

Priya Natarajan: Where to follow along
- Confluence will have PRD + design spec + ADRs once Talia posts links.
- For questions, start in #eng-platform (and tag @Ethan Park / @Logan Wright / @Chloe Martin / @Aditya Rao depending).

Talia Benson: Adding links + concrete asks.

Talia Benson: Docs (internal):
- PRD: confluence/product-docs/requirements/standard-cost-model-prd
- Design spec (API v1): confluence/product-docs/design-specs/cost-model-api-v1-design-spec
- ADR-021 (single canonical spec/API): confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-021-standardize-cost-model-interfaces
- ADR-022 (taxonomy/semantics): confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-022-cost-breakdown-taxonomy

Talia Benson: Immediate asks this week:
1) Please skim ADR-022 and comment if you think any of the breakdown categories will block your use-case (esp prefill/decode split + overhead).
2) If your service currently reports “cost” anywhere, reply in this thread with a link to the code path or dashboard. We’re building an inventory.
3) Runtime folks: we’re going to lock telemetry contracts for KV cache + batch efficiency. If you think any metric is too unstable, flag it now.

Ethan Park: For folks wondering “what is the API actually returning?” v1 endpoints we’re targeting:
- `POST /internal/cost-model/v1/estimate` (per-request estimate w/ breakdown)
- `POST /internal/cost-model/v1/breakdown` (aggregated breakdown for a time window/workload summary, bounded)
- `POST /internal/cost-model/v1/what-if` (projections for config deltas: batching/caching/quant profiles)

Ethan Park: Auth/tenancy will be standard control-plane service auth. We’ll have a shared client lib w/ timeouts + retries + schema version negotiation so Console/Optimize don’t re-roll.

Logan Wright: Telemetry side: we’re standardizing contracts for:
- token accounting split prefill vs decode
- KV cache hit/miss + “effective saved tokens” accounting
- batch efficiency/queueing metrics (so we can attribute scheduler effects)

Logan Wright: Important: the cost model can only be as good as the inputs. We’re adding data quality checks + “telemetry lag” signals so we can degrade gracefully (and not page everyone for late pipelines).

Chloe Martin: Console perspective: we’re planning to adopt behind a feature flag per workspace.
- Fallback behavior: if cost-model API is degraded, Console shows existing breakdown + a small “degraded” banner (so we don’t blank charts).
- We need p95 latency targets because some views are on hot paths.

Aditya Rao: Optimize asks: please keep the what-if endpoint strict + bounded. We’ve had “ROI math” drift because inputs/assumptions weren’t explicit.
- We need to return confidence/uncertainty (even if coarse) + quality guardrails for quant profiles.
- We’re okay punting “cache warm-rate modeling” to v2 if it’s going to explode complexity.

Rafael Mendes: SRE note: we’re going to treat correctness/drift as a first-class SLI alongside availability/latency. That means:
- explicit drift definition (predicted vs observed spend) w/ telemetry lag accounted for
- alerting that doesn’t flap during known backfills
- runbook + feature flag rollback steps

Laura Bennett: Finance view: appreciate the explicit overhead allocation category. Please do not silently “shift” overhead between buckets across versions.
- If the policy changes, we need versioned semantics + a clear effective date.
- For monthly reporting windows, we’ll need stable aggregates even if per-request estimates get better later.

Allison Grant: +1 on determinism. Please avoid “best effort” floating logic changing day-to-day. If a model input is unknown, return it explicitly as unknown/missing + degrade, don’t guess.

Connor O'Brien: Release coordination: can we align on how this ships?
- schema package
- service endpoints
- client lib
- Console + Optimize toggles

Connor O'Brien: If we need to land schema+client first for compile-time typing, call it out so we can plan release trains.

Ethan Park: Re releases: yes, planned order is schema package -> client lib -> service -> adopters.
- Console/Optimize will pin to v1, behind flags.
- We’ll do a staged rollout: staging -> internal dogfood -> small % of prod workspaces -> ramp.

ReleaseBot: Release train note: window for next platform bundle closes in 3d. If you need schema/client in the bundle, please tag @Connor O'Brien with PR links.

Talia Benson: Re: timeline, we’ll post weekly status in #eng-platform and summarize highlights in #eng.

Talia Benson: Also, reminder: this is *internal only* for now. If customer asks, Support will route through Console/CS; we’ll provide a consistent explanation once taxonomy is locked.

Priya Natarajan: Thanks all. Please keep comments on ADRs coming this week. The faster we converge on semantics, the faster Console/Optimize can stop diverging.

Priya Natarajan: If you have concerns about overhead allocation / “what counts”, put it in ADR-022 comments rather than re-litigating in DMs so we have an audit trail.

source.messages:
Dylan Brooks: heads up — we found duplicate event emission on `signup_completed` + `activation_first_request` (looks like it fires twice on some redirect paths). this is skewing the activation funnel + email trigger cohorts. quick patch PR is up, can we get fast reviews?

Dylan Brooks: PR: https://github.com/redwood/redwood/pull/28521 ("Fix duplicate signup event emission")

Dylan Brooks: repro is basically: sign up -> verify email -> back button -> continue -> we emit twice. also seeing it from retry logic on the client.

Dylan Brooks: logs from prod sample (same `event_id` missing, so both pass through):
```
[events] name=signup_completed user_id=usr_8c1... session_id=sess_33a... request_id=req_01HT... ts=...
[events] name=signup_completed user_id=usr_8c1... session_id=sess_33a... request_id=req_01HT... ts=... (+120ms)
```

Nadia Rahman: :eyes: taking a look. do we have an idempotency key now or just dedupe on server by (user_id, name, window)?

Dylan Brooks: server-side dedupe in the ingestion layer. PR adds a `client_event_uuid` + also a short window dedupe for legacy clients. (we can’t rely on request_id bc it can change)

Logan Wright: +1 we need this asap — dashboard is showing weird >100% conversions in a couple cohorts. what’s the dedupe window?

Dylan Brooks: 10s window keyed on (user_id, event_name, client_event_uuid if present else session_id + path). only for the handful of onboarding events.

Ethan Park: reviewing now. does this affect downstream billing/metering or just lifecycle analytics?

Dylan Brooks: just product analytics + lifecycle triggers. metering uses token usage events so no direct $ impact, but it does affect “first_success” segments.

Mei Lin: data side: we also saw this in the warehouse — duplicates started around the console redirect change last week. if this lands today, we should annotate the funnel dataset for the spike window.

Allison Grant: +1 on annotation. also please confirm we’re not dropping legitimate retries (e.g., offline -> resend) with the 10s window.

Dylan Brooks: yep, legitimate retries should have different `client_event_uuid` once SDK update is in. for legacy, 10s is conservative; the duplicates we see are within <1s.

Jada Williams: quick question: how does this interact with experiment cohort assignment? if cohort is set on signup event and we dedupe, are we stable?

Dylan Brooks: cohort assignment happens earlier (server-side at account create). these events are just emitting + used for funnel stages and email triggers.

Benji Okafor: can we ship this behind a flag in ingestion? in case we need to roll back quickly.

Dylan Brooks: PR includes a kill switch: `EVENT_DEDUPE_ONBOARDING_ENABLED` default on for 10% then ramp.

DeployBot: Buildkite build #118402 for PR #28521 started. (<https://buildkite.com/redwood/redwood/builds/118402|view>)

Nadia Rahman: left 2 minor comments (naming + add `source=console` to dedupe key for one event). otherwise LGTM.

Ethan Park: approved. only nit: can we log when we dedupe (count metric) so we can validate drop post-deploy?

Dylan Brooks: good call — added `events.deduped.count` metric + tags (event_name, source). pushed.

DeployBot: Buildkite build #118402 passed. (<https://buildkite.com/redwood/redwood/builds/118402|view>)

Logan Wright: approved from analytics side. pls ping when deployed so we can re-run the sanity queries.

Dylan Brooks: merged. going to request hotfix deploy to prod console + ingestion today if release manager ok.

Benji Okafor: I can take the deploy. what’s the target window?

Dylan Brooks: next 1–2 hrs ideally (before the next email send batch). also FYI marketing asked for confirmation since triggers were noisy.

DeployBot: PR #28521 merged into main by Dylan Brooks.

Allison Grant: once it’s out, I’ll update the activation dashboard annotation + alert thresholds (the duplicates were tripping the “activation jump” alert).

Mei Lin: I’ll backfill a deduped view for the affected date range for the readout, but long-term should be fine once fixed.

Dylan Brooks: thanks all. Benji lmk when deploy is complete; I’ll ping Logan/Nadia for validation.

Benji Okafor: deployed to ingestion at 1768629042. ramped to 10% and watching dedupe metric now.

source.thread:
Ibrahim Khan: Hey #eng — request for broad review this week on two ADRs for the cross-language SDK parity initiative (Python/TS/Go). These will set defaults + behaviors for retries/timeouts/streaming, so we want eyes from SRE/platform/security + anyone who’s been paged by “SDK weirdness”.

Ibrahim Khan: ADRs (comment directly in Confluence pls):
- Retry policy v1: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-SDK-Retry-Policy-v1
- Timeout model: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-SDK-Timeout-Model

Ibrahim Khan: Heads up: there are potentially breaking behavior changes depending on how folks rely on current defaults.

Ibrahim Khan: Proposed highlights:
1) Retries: standardize on retryable = 429, 408, and 5xx (500/502/503/504), plus specific network errors (connection reset, TLS handshake, DNS, etc). Explicitly NOT retry 4xx like 400/401/403/404. Respect `Retry-After` (seconds + HTTP-date).
2) Backoff: exponential w/ jitter, bounded (to avoid retry storms). Max attempts standardized (see ADR).
3) Timeouts: move to a consistent model across SDKs. Overall request timeout + connect + read/idle semantics (language mapping differs but the *meaning* should be consistent).
4) Streaming: conservative: no automatic retry once bytes/events are received (avoid duplicate side effects). We’ll surface partial output + typed error with request_id + guidance for safe retry w/ idempotency keys.

Ibrahim Khan: If you’ve got strong opinions on defaults (esp. max attempts + default timeouts) please comment. We’re trying to avoid both “hang forever” and “retry storm” failure modes.

Monica Patel: +1 — we’re getting real support pain from inconsistencies (“works in TS but not Python”). We need a single standard we can point customers to. Review ask is mainly: are these defaults safe for production + do they align with SRE expectations.

Allison Grant: SRE POV: thank you for flagging in #eng. Biggest concern is default retry behavior under elevated 429s. Please double check:
- honoring Retry-After is mandatory
- max attempts is capped and *small*
- jitter is real jitter (not just +/- 1ms)
- we don’t retry POST-ish calls unless they’re safe/idempotent in practice

Allison Grant: Also: please ensure all SDK errors include request_id. Support/oncall relies on that.

Sean Gallagher: Skimmed retry ADR. Generally aligned.
Two nits:
1) 409: I see it mentioned as “maybe” in the doc. Please keep 409 out of default retries unless we can prove idempotency (or only if idempotency key is present).
2) 408: ok to include, but ensure it doesn’t cause retries on customer-side timeouts (client initiated) — those should be terminal.

Marcus Lin: Security review lens:
- Please call out that auth secret values must never be present in thrown errors or debug logs. Even in “verbose” mode, redact.
- ADR should explicitly state auth precedence order (explicit param > env var). I know that’s in the separate auth doc, but readers will assume it here.

Marcus Lin: For timeouts, please avoid recommending “set timeout=0 to disable”. That pattern tends to leak into production and becomes “hang forever”. If we allow disabling, should be explicit and not the easy path.

Henry Cho: Product angle: breaking changes messaging matters. If defaults change (especially timeouts), we need:
- migration notes w/ “what changed + why”
- snippets in all languages showing how to restore old behavior (if possible)
- clear guidance on streaming retry (people will do naive retries otherwise)

Ethan Park: Platform: +1 on Retry-After respect. We’ve been cleaning up gateway behavior to always emit Retry-After on 429. Please make sure ADR says SDKs must parse both formats.

Ethan Park: Also request_id: we’re standardizing it across streaming + non-streaming responses. SDKs should surface it even when body parse fails.

Dana Lo: TS owner here — we had a bug where our middleware retried some 400s (!!). The ADR fixes that, but it will be noticeable for customers who were inadvertently “succeeding on retry” due to flaky client-side payloads. Might be good to note in migration docs: “we no longer retry 400”.

Sabrina James: Python: timeout ADR looks good. We currently have “timeout set on request” but streaming iterator can block forever in some configs. The new model should fix the hang.

Sabrina James: Question: for streaming, do we want an “inactivity timeout” (no bytes for N seconds) distinct from overall? It’s a common proxy issue.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go: +1 on inactivity/idle timeout for streaming reads. In Go it maps cleanly to ReadTimeout-ish + ctx deadlines.

Connor O'Brien: Release coordination: pls keep anything default-changing behind a flag for one minor release if possible, then flip defaults next. We can coordinate announcements across 3 SDKs.

Wei Chen: Docs: once defaults are final, I need the exact recommended numbers and the exact option names per language. Please try not to change them after we start updating docs/quickstarts.

Sahana Iyer: Conformance tests: we can add a fixture for “stream emits some events then disconnects” and assert: (a) no auto retry, (b) typed error includes request_id if present, (c) partial output accessible.

Talia Benson: Program mgmt note: to hit the 8-week timeline, can we get reviews by EOW? We’re blocking implementation work until these ADRs are “v1 accepted”.

Ibrahim Khan: Thanks all — action items I’m taking from this thread:
- Remove 409 from default retry set (or make it explicit opt-in only; likely opt-in only w/ idempotency key)
- Clarify 408 handling: only server-side 408; client-side timeout is terminal
- Add explicit note in timeout ADR about “no timeout” not being the recommended config (and how to safely disable if we must)
- Add streaming inactivity timeout section (separate from overall) + how it maps across languages
- Ensure migration notes call out: “no longer retry 400” + “streaming no auto-retry once bytes received”

Ibrahim Khan: Please keep comments on the ADR pages too so we have an audit trail. I’ll incorporate changes tomorrow and ping for final signoff.

Confluence Bot: Ibrahim Khan updated page “ADR: SDK Retry Policy v1” https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-SDK-Retry-Policy-v1 (added: 409 default behavior clarification, 408 note)

Sean Gallagher: Left comments. Once those edits land, SRE is +1.

Marcus Lin: Left comments on logging/redaction language. Once updated, security is +1.

Monica Patel: Appreciate the quick turn. We’ll post in #announcements once the ADRs are final + link migration notes for customers.

source.body:
Talia Benson: hey folks — looking for reviewers this week for the main Optimize quality gating PR chain. trying to keep this moving so we can hit internal beta in Jan.

Talia Benson: dependency chain (roughly):
1) config diff schema + static validation (redwood)
2) eval-harness trigger endpoint + retry/flake aggregation (eval-harness)
3) optimize service wiring + gate state machine + enforcement on apply (redwood)
4) console/api surfacing + UI blocks + override flow (redwood)

Talia Benson: PRs (please grab what you can):
- redwood PR-19511 add config-diff schema + validation rules
- eval-harness PR-412 trigger endpoint for Optimize gating
- eval-harness PR-418 flake score + rerun policy
- redwood PR-19502 gate-run state machine + persistence
- redwood PR-19523 wire Optimize -> eval-harness, retries/timeouts/artifacts
- redwood PR-19531 block apply when gate fails + surface reasons
- redwood PR-19537 override flow (expiring) + audit events
- redwood PR-19570 add artifact links in Console API

Talia Benson: reviewers needed esp for: runtime safety (batching/cache knobs), eval rigor, and “enforcement lives in the right place” (defense in depth).

Aditya Rao: +1 — if you can only do one pass, focus on 19531 (apply boundary) and 19537 (overrides). those are the ones we’ll get hammered on in customer/security reviews.

Jordan Lee: I can take 19531 + 19502 (state machine) from policy/enforcement angle. any gotchas called out from architecture thread? want to make sure we’re not relying on Console-only enforcement.

Talia Benson: yep, requirement is defense-in-depth: Optimize API must block apply, rollout controller will also block promotion later. Console is just a UI surface.

Benji Okafor: I’ll review 19511. quick q: does schema include per-change-type validation or is it just structural? (e.g., disallow cache+batch setting combos we know are unsafe)

Noah Patel: we added a minimal set of static checks in the PR description but likely need more. I’ll leave comments w/ a “safe-by-default” list vs “requires stricter gates”.

Mateo Alvarez: I can review 19511 + 19523 for runtime mapping. I want to confirm config diff -> runtime knobs is unambiguous and we’re logging the resolved effective config that eval ran against.

Olivia Grant: I’ll take eval-harness PR-412. main things I’m watching: input contract (baseline selection IDs, prompt set IDs), artifact pointers, and making sure we have a stable “result schema” for Console.

Jonas Weber: I can review PR-418 for flake scoring / reruns. please tag me if there’s any “pass on first run” logic — we need min sample sizes + aggregation rules to avoid false confidence.

Sahana Iyer: I can take a look at 19523 for timeouts/retry behavior. we should align w/ CI plan + GPU scarcity. do we have backoff caps per tier (smoke vs full)?

Connor O'Brien: +1, also want to ensure we don’t block merge queues forever. please include a clear “what fails fast vs what is async” note in the PRs.

Dylan Brooks: I’ll review 19570 (Console API) from tooling side. want to confirm we’re returning a compact summary + stable artifact links, not huge payloads.

Chloe Martin: I can pair w/ Dylan on 19570. also want to sanity check failure reasons are user-readable and map to UI copy (blocked, override requested, override granted/expired).

Kenji Nakamura: if there are UI strings already in 19531/19570, please @ me. I’m trying to keep wording consistent: “Quality gate failed” vs “insufficient confidence” vs “eval suite flaky/quarantined”.

Allison Grant: I’ll skim 19523/19531 for logging/metrics hooks. if we’re returning failure reasons, we should emit the same structured reason codes for dashboards/alerts.

Sergio Costa: +1 — especially distinguishing “real regression” vs “eval infra timeout” vs “validation rejected”. otherwise alerts will be noisy.

Marissa Cole: can we add a short checklist in the root PR descriptions? 
- gate run persisted
- artifacts link recorded
- apply boundary enforcement
- override + audit
- console surface
Helps product sign-off.

Elliot Price: once we’re close, I can start pulling notes for docs. if the override flow is changing (approvers/expiration), please keep the PR description updated so external docs don’t drift.

Talia Benson: amazing, thanks. I’ll drop assignments + due dates in Linear and link back here. also: if you see missing tests, please call it out early — we’ve got an E2E test PR planned but want to avoid whack-a-mole late.

Jordan Lee: claiming 19531 now. will leave comments today.

Benji Okafor: claimed 19511. will focus on schema stability + validation reason codes.

Olivia Grant: starting on 412. if anyone has opinions on baseline identifier format (route/model/hw sku), send them my way.

Jonas Weber: on 418. will pay attention to aggregation + how we store per-run variance.

Talia Benson: :thumbsup: thanks all — tagging owners directly on each PR now.

2) eval-harness trigger endpoint + retry/flake aggregation (eval-harness)
3) optimize service wiring + gate state machine + enforcement on apply (redwood)
4) console/api surfacing + UI blocks + override flow (redwood)

Talia Benson: PRs (please grab what you can):
- redwood PR-19511 add config-diff schema + validation rules
- eval-harness PR-412 trigger endpoint for Optimize gating
- eval-harness PR-418 flake score + rerun policy
- redwood PR-19502 gate-run state machine + persistence
- redwood PR-19523 wire Optimize -> eval-harness, retries/timeouts/artifacts
- redwood PR-19531 block apply when gate fails + surface reasons
- redwood PR-19537 override flow (expiring) + audit events
- redwood PR-19570 add artifact links in Console API

Talia Benson: reviewers needed esp for: runtime safety (batching/cache knobs), eval rigor, and “enforcement lives in the right place” (defense in depth).

Aditya Rao: +1 — if you can only do one pass, focus on 19531 (apply boundary) and 19537 (overrides). those are the ones we’ll get hammered on in customer/security reviews.

Jordan Lee: I can take 19531 + 19502 (state machine) from policy/enforcement angle. any gotchas called out from architecture thread? want to make sure we’re not relying on Console-only enforcement.

Talia Benson: yep, requirement is defense-in-depth: Optimize API must block apply, rollout controller will also block promotion later. Console is just a UI surface.

Benji Okafor: I’ll review 19511. quick q: does schema include per-change-type validation or is it just structural? (e.g., disallow cache+batch setting combos we know are unsafe)

Noah Patel: we added a minimal set of static checks in the PR description but likely need more. I’ll leave comments w/ a “safe-by-default” list vs “requires stricter gates”.

Mateo Alvarez: I can review 19511 + 19523 for runtime mapping. I want to confirm config diff -> runtime knobs is unambiguous and we’re logging the resolved effective config that eval ran against.

Olivia Grant: I’ll take eval-harness PR-412. main things I’m watching: input contract (baseline selection IDs, prompt set IDs), artifact pointers, and making sure we have a stable “result schema” for Console.

Jonas Weber: I can review PR-418 for flake scoring / reruns. please tag me if there’s any “pass on first run” logic — we need min sample sizes + aggregation rules to avoid false confidence.

Sahana Iyer: I can take a look at 19523 for timeouts/retry behavior. we should align w/ CI plan + GPU scarcity. do we have backoff caps per tier (smoke vs full)?

Connor O'Brien: +1, also want to ensure we don’t block merge queues forever. please include a clear “what fails fast vs what is async” note in the PRs.

Dylan Brooks: I’ll review 19570 (Console API) from tooling side. want to confirm we’re returning a compact summary + stable artifact links, not huge payloads.

Chloe Martin: I can pair w/ Dylan on 19570. also want to sanity check failure reasons are user-readable and map to UI copy (blocked, override requested, override granted/expired).

Kenji Nakamura: if there are UI strings already in 19531/19570, please @ me. I’m trying to keep wording consistent: “Quality gate failed” vs “insufficient confidence” vs “eval suite flaky/quarantined”.

Allison Grant: I’ll skim 19523/19531 for logging/metrics hooks. if we’re returning failure reasons, we should emit the same structured reason codes for dashboards/alerts.

Sergio Costa: +1 — especially distinguishing “real regression” vs “eval infra timeout” vs “validation rejected”. otherwise alerts will be noisy.

Marissa Cole: can we add a short checklist in the root PR descriptions? 
- gate run persisted
- artifacts link recorded
- apply boundary enforcement
- override + audit
- console surface
Helps product sign-off.

Elliot Price: once we’re close, I can start pulling notes for docs. if the override flow is changing (approvers/expiration), please keep the PR description updated so external docs don’t drift.

Talia Benson: amazing, thanks. I’ll drop assignments + due dates in Linear and link back here. also: if you see missing tests, please call it out early — we’ve got an E2E test PR planned but want to avoid whack-a-mole late.

Jordan Lee: claiming 19531 now. will leave comments today.

Benji Okafor: claimed 19511. will focus on schema stability + validation reason codes.

Olivia Grant: starting on 412. if anyone has opinions on baseline identifier format (route/model/hw sku), send them my way.

Jonas Weber: on 418. will pay attention to aggregation + how we store per-run variance.

Talia Benson: :thumbsup: thanks all — tagging owners directly on each PR now.

source.content:
Ethan Park: quick cross-team q before we lock behavior: should the installer *block install* if bundle signature validates but the signing key isn’t in our trust store ("unknown key")? (thinking rotation + older installer cases)

Connor O'Brien: my bias: yes, block. otherwise attacker can sign with any key and we’re effectively doing "integrity but no provenance".

Marcus Lin: +1 block by default. unknown key == untrusted root. if we allow it, we’re building an easy downgrade of trust.

Vivek Kulkarni: product angle: blocking is correct long-term, but we need a migration story that doesn’t brick existing Private customers who are slow to update the installer in air-gapped envs.

Fatima Noor: +1 on migration. in pilots, some customers update infra quarterly. if a bundle arrives signed w/ a new key and they’re on an old installer, they’ll be dead in the water.

Sahana Iyer: for release train: we can avoid this by pinning signing key for a while and only rotating when we’ve confirmed installers in the field have both keys.

Ethan Park: right now PR has behavior:
- invalid signature => hard fail
- unknown key => hard fail
- missing signature (legacy) => hard fail unless `--allow-unsigned` (break-glass)
unknown key is the contentious one.

Dev Patel: support POV: hard fail is ok if the error is super explicit and tells them exactly what to do. right now "untrusted key" tends to become a 3-day ticket.

Owen Phillips: +1. we need a deterministic path like "update trust store file" not "reinstall newer installer" (which can be painful offline)

Jordan Lee: security semantics: if you allow unknown key with just a warning, you’re basically allowing a self-signed bundle. if we do allow *anything*, it must be a break-glass path with audit trail.

Sean Gallagher: ops: what’s the failure mode in the field? older installer + newer bundle after key rollover. can we detect and provide actionable remediation w/o network?

Ethan Park: proposed remediation options:
1) ship installer with embedded trust store containing CURRENT + NEXT keys
2) allow `--trust-store /path/to/trust-store.json` to inject new public keys offline
3) break-glass flag `--verification-mode=permissive` (name TBD) that still logs a giant warning

Marcus Lin: (2) is the right model. but we need policy: who provides the trust-store.json and how does customer know it’s legit? it becomes another artifact that must be distributed securely.

Connor O'Brien: we can publish a "trust roots" package alongside releases (signed by the *old* key). in rotation:
- release N: installer trusts key A; trust-roots package signed w/ A includes key B
- customer imports trust-roots package offline
- release N+1: bundles signed with B

Logan Wright: if we do any override, we should emit audit events locally:
- verify_success
- verify_failed_unknown_key
- verify_override_unknown_key_used
with redaction-safe fields (key fingerprint, bundle build id, etc.)

Vivek Kulkarni: +1, but please don’t make this a 10-step ceremony for customers. we need a simple doc: "copy file X to Y and rerun preflight".

Fatima Noor: customers will ask "how do I validate the trust roots package". if it’s signed by the previous key and verified by their existing installer, that’s a good story.

Sahana Iyer: from CI side: rotation plan is doable, but we need to be strict about not switching signing key until the trust-roots package is published + docs updated.

Dev Patel: can we improve the error text to something like:
"Bundle signed by unknown key fingerprint <fp>. Your installer trusts: <fp1>, <fp2>. Update trust store (see docs) or use break-glass with approval."

Owen Phillips: also include where logs are and what to attach to support bundle.

Ethan Park: makes sense. question: do we *ever* allow permissive mode for unknown key, or do we require trust store update as the only path?

Marcus Lin: my position: default hard fail. allow override only if:
- explicit `--break-glass` + `--reason` (or env var) AND
- writes an audit marker AND
- does NOT persist trust (i.e., doesn’t add the key to trust store automatically)
so it’s painful on purpose.

Sean Gallagher: +1. we don’t want a lingering permissive setting that people forget.

Connor O'Brien: release concern: break-glass shouldn’t become a de facto workaround for our mistakes (like forgetting to ship trust roots). add gating checks on release side.

Sahana Iyer: +1, we can add a release gate: when new key is introduced, pipeline must publish trust-roots package signed by previous key, and verification tests must pass with (a) old installer + updated trust store, (b) new installer.

Jordan Lee: we should codify "unknown key" as distinct error code. something like `RPI_VERIFY_003_UNKNOWN_TRUST_ROOT` vs `RPI_VERIFY_002_INVALID_SIG`.

Ethan Park: ok. what about *existing* customers with installer versions that don’t support external trust stores yet? (old installer can’t import new trust roots package)

Vivek Kulkarni: then we should not rotate until we’ve reached a floor version in the field (or we ship an out-of-band small trust-store updater tool that works on older installs).

Fatima Noor: for air-gapped, forcing a full installer upgrade is sometimes ok, but we need to coordinate w/ SEs. can we commit to a long overlap window? (e.g., 6+ months)

Connor O'Brien: overlap window yes. also the initial key should be stable for a while. rotation is rare unless compromised.

Marcus Lin: emergency compromise is the scary case. in that case, you may have to force upgrade / new trust roots quickly. break-glass can exist but should require security approval (per policy).

Logan Wright: if compromise case: we should log "revoked key used" as separate event too (and block).

Dev Patel: for support runbooks: we need a decision tree:
- unknown key -> update trust store
- invalid signature -> treat as tampering/transfer corruption
- missing signature -> legacy bundle / break-glass only

Ethan Park: sounds like consensus:
1) block on unknown key by default
2) support offline trust store update path (preferred)
3) break-glass override exists but is gated + loud + audited, does not auto-trust
I’ll update installer behavior + error text accordingly.

Connor O'Brien: +1. please loop me on exact CLI flags so release docs match.

Marcus Lin: +1, and share the proposed error copy w/ security before merge.

Vivek Kulkarni: +1. also add a short "migration" section in docs: what changes for existing customers and what they need to do before key rotation.

Ethan Park: will do. I’ll draft wording + flag names and drop in #eng-platform for quick review.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: once implemented, SRE wants a quick "what pages" note for on-call (unknown key spike will show up as install failures).

Logan Wright: I’ll align audit event naming w/ existing schema and make sure support bundle includes key fingerprint + verify result.

Dev Patel: thanks. this is one of those things that saves 20 tickets later.

source.text:
Jae Kim: quick q on the status page component mapping work — for route/capability tagging, what’s the expected labels/values? i’m looking at gateway metrics + a couple workers and want to make sure we tag in the “approved” way so it actually maps to public components.

Logan Wright: yup, we standardized on 3 dims for anything that can drive status/public summaries:
- `component_id`
- `region`
- `capability`
This is the same set incident-bot is collecting in intake now.

Jae Kim: ok but what counts as “capability” vs “route”? e.g. `/v1/chat/completions` + stream=true is that “chat” or “streaming”? + do we include model family anywhere?

Mina Haddad: capability is intentionally coarse + user-visible. don’t put model names in tags.
Rules of thumb:
- `/v1/chat/completions` (non-stream) => `capability=chat`
- `/v1/chat/completions` with `stream=true` OR SSE endpoint => `capability=streaming`
- `/v1/embeddings` => `capability=embeddings`
- `/v1/rerank` => `capability=reranking`
If something is “internal route-ish” (scheduler calls, prefill service), keep that as internal labels but still map outward to a capability.

Christine Wong: +1. also: `component_id` is product surface, not service. for almost all request-path metrics in prod it should be `component_id=hosted_api` (Dedicated/Private are different; we can go over those separately).

Allison Grant: docs pointer: Confluence has the taxonomy + definitions: “Status page component taxonomy” (SRE → status-page-and-comms). the canonical ids are lowercase snake_case. pls don’t invent new ones ad-hoc.

Jae Kim: do we have a schema somewhere i can validate against? i keep fat-fingering region names.

Logan Wright: yep, schema is in the registry validation PR. the “single source of truth” is a JSON registry file (and a JSON schema that CI validates). you can search in the observability-pack repo for “status component registry schema”.

Caleb Johnson: regions should match what routing uses today (no cloud provider AZs). examples we’re using:
- `us-east`
- `us-west`
- `eu-west`
- `ap-southeast`
If you only know “global” (rare), we’d rather you plumb region through than tag `global`.

Devon Price: for incident-bot intake fields we also use those exact strings. so if you tag metrics with `region=us-east` and pick `capability=streaming`, the bot drafts will line up with the component options.

Jae Kim: can someone drop an example tag block for gateway?

Christine Wong: from the gateway side (pseudo):
```python
metrics.increment(
  "gateway.request",
  tags={
    "component_id": "hosted_api",
    "region": request.region,  # e.g. "us-east"
    "capability": capability_from_request(request),
  },
)
```
`capability_from_request` should be based on endpoint + stream flag. not on model.

Mina Haddad: and if you’re doing this in traces too, same keys. we want to be able to pivot dashboards + summaries by the same dims.

Allison Grant: re: “route” — if you really need it for debugging, keep it as `route_name` or `endpoint` internally, but do not use it as a public-facing grouping. we had the “API ok but embeddings failing” issue because things were tagged as generic API without the capability split.

Jae Kim: makes sense. what about dedicated control plane? i’m touching a metric in the capacity scheduler.

Rafael Mendes: dedicated control plane is a separate component_id (not `hosted_api`). the taxonomy doc calls it out as “Dedicated: Control Plane” and we’re exposing a limited set of signals publicly. if your metric affects customer-facing provisioning/scale actions, tag it to that. if it’s purely internal noise, don’t.

Devon Price: + note: dedicated/private incidents have comms constraints. don’t include customer identifiers in tags/labels. component_id + region + capability is safe.

Elliot Price: if you need “what do we say publicly” guidance, docs side has a short section under incident templates. but for tagging, stick to the schema + examples above.

Jae Kim: awesome. i’ll implement with `capability=streaming` on chat stream path + keep `route_name` internal. will ping if CI yells at me.

Christine Wong: :thumbsup: if CI fails, it’ll usually point at invalid enum value. easiest fix is copy/paste from the registry.

Logan Wright: also worth checking the hotfix PR we landed last week (misclassified embeddings vs chat). the failure mode was exactly “wrong capability tag → wrong component update”. so thank you for being careful here 🙏

source.messages:
Rafael Mendes: QuantaLedger private rollout: customer wants US-only residency but has EU subcontractor on the rollout. Security ok if its metadata-only + audited. Can we set up a least-priv role?
Jonas Weber: Yep. Well add a role for cluster read-only + limited console views. No payload access. Tracking in SUP-4521.
Aisha Rahman: Make it explicit: no break-glass for contractor. Also confirm audit export is available for exception window.
Hiro Tanaka: From SE side, what does the contractor need? Usually just health, deploy status, and admin audit log view.
Rafael Mendes: Great. Ill update customer once exception record is published.

source.text:
Priya Natarajan: We’re close to flipping the oncall handoff for the PLG signal router. Can folks drop the *must-have* metrics + alert conditions? Thinking HubSpot 429s, retries, dropped events, lag, etc. Also owners + dashboard link placeholders so we can wire this into PagerDuty.

Logan Wright: +1. From telemetry/analytics side, the top things that will bite us:
- event lag (telemetry -> analytics table -> scoring output)
- aggregator job failures / partial writes
- dropped events / DLQ growth
If we don’t alert on lag, sales will just see “nothing happening” and assume routing is broken.

Mei Lin: For the daily aggregation job (ENG-7732 / PR-28458), we should alert on:
- `aggregation_job_success` (1/0) per run
- `aggregation_rows_written` (unexpectedly low vs trailing 7d baseline)
- `aggregation_backfill_runs` (if backfill is running, suppress some lag alerts)
Also a gauge for `analytics_table_partition_freshness_hours`.

Caleb Johnson: Ingestion/data quality:
- `telemetry_event_ingest_lag_seconds` p95 and max
- `telemetry_events_dropped_total` (with reason label: schema_invalid, auth, throttled)
- `telemetry_events_dlq_depth`
We should page only on sustained DLQ growth + lag, not on occasional schema_invalid spikes (docs instrumentation can be noisy).

Ethan Park: HubSpot integration metrics (PR-28503 + PR-28544):
- `hubspot_requests_total` by endpoint
- `hubspot_429s_total` + `hubspot_rate_limited_seconds_total`
- `hubspot_5xx_total`
- `hubspot_retries_total` + `hubspot_retry_queue_depth`
- `hubspot_task_create_failures_total`
- `hubspot_company_upsert_failures_total`
Also *idempotency* issues are subtle — a counter for `hubspot_deduped_updates_total` would help validate we’re not spamming timeline events.

Nadia Rahman: Scoring service should expose:
- `signals_created_total` (by signal_type)
- `signals_suppressed_total` (by suppression_reason)
- `signals_routed_total` (by destination=hubspot)
- `signals_failed_total` (validation_failed, missing_company_mapping, hubspot_error)
And a histogram: `signal_end_to_end_latency_seconds` (telemetry timestamp -> task created)

Marcus Lin: From security side: please add a metric + alert if we ever attempt to send disallowed fields to HubSpot.
Like `hubspot_payload_policy_violations_total` (should be 0 always). If >0, page the oncall + auto-disable routing.

Talia Benson: Capturing this for the runbook. Can we agree on *alert thresholds* (page vs ticket)? Proposed:
P0 page:
- HubSpot 429s sustained (>= 10% of requests for 10m) OR retry queue depth continuously increasing for 15m
- End-to-end lag p95 > 6h (excluding backfills)
- Task create failures > 2% for 10m
P1 ticket:
- 5xx spikes but recovering
- row writes drop >50% vs 7d median
Thoughts?

Logan Wright: 6h lag feels high given we’re doing daily windows, but the expectation from sales is “I should see tasks same day.” I’d set:
- ticket at p95 > 2h for 2 consecutive hours
- page at p95 > 6h for 2 consecutive hours
and explicitly suppress during backfill.

Mei Lin: +1 to suppression during backfills. We can tag runs with `window_type=daily|backfill` and key alerts off daily only.
Also add:
- `aggregation_window_complete_timestamp` gauge (epoch) so we can compute freshness in Grafana easily.

Ethan Park: On 429s: HubSpot rate limits are bursty. I’d rather page on “routing stopped” symptoms:
- `hubspot_task_create_success_rate` < 95% for 15m (page)
- `hubspot_retry_queue_depth` > 5k and increasing (page)
And a warning when 429 ratio > 5% for 10m (ticket) so we tune batching.

Caleb Johnson: For dropped events: page if `telemetry_events_dropped_total{reason!=schema_invalid}` increases by > 1k in 10m AND lag p95 > 1h. Otherwise we’ll page every time a client SDK rolls.

Priya Natarajan: This is great. Let’s lock a minimal set for day-1 oncall:
1) HubSpot delivery health (success rate, retries, 429/5xx)
2) E2E lag
3) DLQ depth / dropped events
4) Job/run success
Anything beyond that can be phase 2.

Nadia Rahman: Please also include a “volume sanity” panel:
- `signals_created_total` by type
- `signals_routed_total` by region
If we suddenly double our routing volume, that’s usually a threshold regression.

Talia Benson: Owners?
- HubSpot metrics/alerts: @Ethan Park
- Ingestion lag/DLQ/drops: @Caleb Johnson
- Aggregation freshness + writes: @Mei Lin
- Overall dashboards + PD integration: I can coordinate but need a DRI for the service

Priya Natarajan: DRI for service ops is Logan (Telemetry & Analytics) for this pilot. Ethan owns HubSpot path. I’ll be escalation for platform.

source.messages:
Connor O'Brien: Heads up: airgap bundle from the latest private installer build is missing the Grafana dashboards for the Private observability min pack. On-prem install comes up, Prometheus scrapes OK, but Grafana has *zero* Redwood dashboards.

Fatima Noor: Confirmed on my side. Using the onprem offline flow (no egress). Grafana is reachable, datasource is configured, but dashboard folder is empty. I only see default Grafana stuff.

Diego Santos: Ugh. Is it just dashboards or also alert rules?

Fatima Noor: Alerts *seem* present (I see the PrometheusRule objects). The missing bit looks like the dashboard JSONs aren’t in the airgap tarball at all.

Dylan Brooks: That matches what I saw in staging earlier this week. The Helm chart references dashboards via ConfigMaps, but the installer’s airgap bundle needs to include the `observability-pack` dashboards directory (or the generated ConfigMap manifests). Might be a manifest omission in the bundler.

Chloe Martin: Quick check: are we expecting the installer to ship raw dashboard JSONs and render ConfigMaps at install-time, or ship pre-rendered ConfigMaps? I thought we went with “ship raw dashboards + grafana sidecar loads them”.

Diego Santos: For Private installer we’re packaging the raw JSON artifacts + the helm bits. If those JSONs aren’t in the bundle, nothing to mount, so sidecar has nothing to load.

Connor O'Brien: I unpacked the artifact and searched:
`tar -tf redwood-private-airgap-bundle.tgz | grep -i grafana`
Nothing except the Grafana image tar.

Rafael Mendes: That explains the Support thread we saw last week (“dashboards blank after upgrade”). If this is the same root cause, we need a hotfix fast.

Sahana Iyer: CI didn’t catch this because smoke tests run in connected env and pull dashboards from the repo path. Airgap path uses the bundle manifest only. We should add an assertion that the bundle contains at least N dashboard JSON files.

Logan Wright: +1. Also can we verify the bundle includes the alert rules + recording rules too? Would rather not fix dashboards and then find rules missing.

Connor O'Brien: Root cause looks like `airgap-bundle-manifest.yaml` doesn’t include the dashboards/rules artifacts from `observability-pack` (only includes images + helm charts). Probably got dropped in the last refactor.

Dylan Brooks: I can take a look at the bundler config. It should include:
- `observability-pack/private-minimum/dashboards/*.json`
- `observability-pack/private-minimum/prometheus-rules/*.yaml`
And then installer should copy into the expected `/var/lib/redwood/observability/` path.

Chloe Martin: If we add raw dashboards, make sure we preserve folder structure so Grafana sidecar labels/folders still work (Runtime vs Control Plane vs Infra). Otherwise everything lands in “General”.

Diego Santos: Also ensure checksums update. Airgap customers verify SHA256 from the manifest.

Connor O'Brien: Proposed plan:
1) Patch installer bundle manifest to include dashboards + rules
2) Cut a hotfix PR + backport to release branch
3) Trigger airgap build + rerun onprem validation plan (at least: install, upgrade, confirm dashboards load)

Rafael Mendes: For severity: I’d call this a release-blocker for on-prem. Without dashboards the “minimum pack” story is broken.

Fatima Noor: Agree. For regulated customers, they specifically asked for turnkey dashboards in offline env. This is exactly the failure mode.

Sahana Iyer: I’ll add a CI step to the installer pipeline:
- unpack bundle
- `find . -name "*.json" | grep dashboards | wc -l` must be > 0
- and maybe validate presence of a known dashboard UID/title file

Elliot Price: Once fixed, can someone drop me the exact version + any user-facing notes? Docs currently say “dashboards included in offline bundle” so we should add a known-issue note for the broken versions + the fixed version.

Connor O'Brien: Dylan can you own the PR? I’ll coordinate the backport + release note.

Dylan Brooks: Yep. I’ll open a PR to `redwood-private-installer` updating the airgap manifest + post-install validation to fail loudly if dashboards directory is missing.

Diego Santos: Please add a preflight warning too. Something like: “observability artifacts not present in bundle; dashboards will be empty” so we don’t silently ship a broken pack.

Chloe Martin: Can we also add a quick postinstall check that Grafana has loaded at least one dashboard ConfigMap? Even a simple “list ConfigMaps with grafana_dashboard=1 label” would help.

Dylan Brooks: Good call. I’ll add a postinstall validation step:
`kubectl -n <ns> get cm -l grafana_dashboard=1 | grep redwood || exit 1`

Logan Wright: While you’re in there, make sure rules path is included too. We had a similar miss earlier where recording rules weren’t copied.

Dylan Brooks: Will do. I’ll verify both dashboard JSONs and rule YAMLs are bundled.

CI Bot: Build notification: nightly private-installer airgap job #482 succeeded (artifact uploaded).

Rafael Mendes: Nightly succeeded but doesn’t validate contents. Let’s not rely on that.

Connor O'Brien: Tracking: Dylan PR today, backport tomorrow AM, then Fatima to re-run the onprem airgap validation checklist.

Fatima Noor: I can rerun as soon as we have a new bundle. I’ll test:
- fresh airgap install
- upgrade from previous broken bundle -> fixed bundle
- confirm dashboards render and alerts present

Elliot Price: Please capture screenshots or at least the list of dashboards showing up. Helps for Support enablement.

Dylan Brooks: PR incoming in ~1 hour.

Connor O'Brien: Thanks all. Let’s keep this thread updated with the PR link + the fixed build ID once it’s cut.

Diego Santos: Also ensure checksums update. Airgap customers verify SHA256 from the manifest.

Connor O'Brien: Proposed plan:
1) Patch installer bundle manifest to include dashboards + rules
2) Cut a hotfix PR + backport to release branch
3) Trigger airgap build + rerun onprem validation plan (at least: install, upgrade, confirm dashboards load)

Rafael Mendes: For severity: I’d call this a release-blocker for on-prem. Without dashboards the “minimum pack” story is broken.

Fatima Noor: Agree. For regulated customers, they specifically asked for turnkey dashboards in offline env. This is exactly the failure mode.

Sahana Iyer: I’ll add a CI step to the installer pipeline:
- unpack bundle
- `find . -name "*.json" | grep dashboards | wc -l` must be > 0
- and maybe validate presence of a known dashboard UID/title file

Elliot Price: Once fixed, can someone drop me the exact version + any user-facing notes? Docs currently say “dashboards included in offline bundle” so we should add a known-issue note for the broken versions + the fixed version.

Connor O'Brien: Dylan can you own the PR? I’ll coordinate the backport + release note.

Dylan Brooks: Yep. I’ll open a PR to `redwood-private-installer` updating the airgap manifest + post-install validation to fail loudly if dashboards directory is missing.

Diego Santos: Please add a preflight warning too. Something like: “observability artifacts not present in bundle; dashboards will be empty” so we don’t silently ship a broken pack.

Chloe Martin: Can we also add a quick postinstall check that Grafana has loaded at least one dashboard ConfigMap? Even a simple “list ConfigMaps with grafana_dashboard=1 label” would help.

Dylan Brooks: Good call. I’ll add a postinstall validation step:
`kubectl -n <ns> get cm -l grafana_dashboard=1 | grep redwood || exit 1`

Logan Wright: While you’re in there, make sure rules path is included too. We had a similar miss earlier where recording rules weren’t copied.

Dylan Brooks: Will do. I’ll verify both dashboard JSONs and rule YAMLs are bundled.

CI Bot: Build notification: nightly private-installer airgap job #482 succeeded (artifact uploaded).

Rafael Mendes: Nightly succeeded but doesn’t validate contents. Let’s not rely on that.

Connor O'Brien: Tracking: Dylan PR today, backport tomorrow AM, then Fatima to re-run the onprem airgap validation checklist.

Fatima Noor: I can rerun as soon as we have a new bundle. I’ll test:
- fresh airgap install
- upgrade from previous broken bundle -> fixed bundle
- confirm dashboards render and alerts present

Elliot Price: Please capture screenshots or at least the list of dashboards showing up. Helps for Support enablement.

Dylan Brooks: PR incoming in ~1 hour.

Connor O'Brien: Thanks all. Let’s keep this thread updated with the PR link + the fixed build ID once it’s cut.

source.content:
Connor O'Brien: Hey folks — working on ENG-19153 preflight checks for the Private installer (observability bits). What should we include in v1, and which failures should be hard-blocking vs WARN-only?

Diego Santos: +1. We got burned a couple times by “install succeeded” but Prometheus never scraped anything b/c RBAC/CRDs missing.

Rafael Mendes: From oncall perspective: hard-block anything that makes the observability pack unusable + causes confusing day-2. If we can detect it, block.

Logan Wright: Agree, but careful: some customers bring their own Prometheus/Grafana and only want our dashboards/rules. Preflight needs to understand mode.

Connor O'Brien: Yeah we have 3 modes in values right now:
- `observability.enabled=true` (we deploy prom/grafana stack)
- `observability.enabled=true` + `prometheus.external=true` (we only ship ServiceMonitors/Rules + dashboards)
- `observability.enabled=false` (no checks)

Allison Grant: Please align with standards doc: minimum pack assumes Prometheus-compatible scraping + rules evaluation. If operator CRDs are required, missing CRDs should be FAIL.

Chloe Martin: For Grafana: we’ve seen empty dashboards because datasource secret/configmap got created but Grafana couldn’t read it (namespace mismatch) or provisioning sidecar disabled. Preflight could at least validate required values + RBAC to create ConfigMaps.

Dylan Brooks: Concrete list proposal (split by component):
1) Cluster prerequisites
- K8s version supported (we’ve had 1.23 vs 1.27 weirdness)
- storage class exists + default storage class if we’re creating PVCs
- can create namespaces + cluster-scoped resources (CRDs/ClusterRole)
2) Prometheus
- if deploying kube-prometheus-stack: ensure `monitoring.coreos.com` CRDs not already in conflicting version
- if external prom: ensure ServiceMonitor/PrometheusRule CRDs exist OR we fall back to annotations
3) Grafana
- if deploying: ensure PVC can be created, and ingress/service type is valid
4) Logs
- if deploying collector: ensure it can read pods (RBAC) + node logs if we’re using daemonset

Tomáš Novák: + runbook links in failure messages pls. Even preflight output should have “what to do next” copy/paste.

Connor O'Brien: 👍. Let’s get specific on what’s FAIL vs WARN.

Rafael Mendes: My vote:
FAIL:
- installer SA doesn’t have required RBAC (cannot create CRDs/ClusterRole/RoleBinding in target namespaces)
- cannot create PVC (no default StorageClass) when deploying prometheus/grafana/loki with persistence enabled
- required CRDs missing in external-prom mode (ServiceMonitor/PodMonitor/PrometheusRule)
- image pull secret missing (air-gapped) when `global.imageRegistry` set
WARN:
- no IngressController detected (only if they set ingress=true)
- storage size below recommended (still works but will page later)
- prometheus retention < 24h

Marcus Lin: Add FAIL for “cluster blocks hostPath mounts” if log collector uses hostPath (some hardened clusters disallow). Or at least detect PodSecurityAdmission restricted mode and warn.

Diego Santos: PSA restricted mode detection is tricky but we can check namespace labels:
`pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted` etc.
If collector DS needs privileged/hostPath -> FAIL unless they’ve set `logs.enabled=false`.

Logan Wright: Also check cardinality constraints? Not really preflight, but we can ensure `metrics.routeLabelEnabled=false` etc for private defaults. Maybe just warn if they try to enable high-card labels.

Allison Grant: +1 warn if they override label config beyond bounds.

Sahana Iyer: From CI failures: we should hard-fail if Prometheus can’t discover any scrape targets due to NetworkPolicy, BUT that’s not preflight — that’s postinstall validation. Preflight can only check “networkpolicy.enabled” and if they’re using default deny w/out explicit allow.

Connor O'Brien: We can do a best-effort: detect if namespace has default deny NP and no allow rules for Prometheus -> WARN w/ guidance.

Dylan Brooks: Another common issue: people install external Prometheus but it’s in a different namespace and they scope RBAC so it can’t watch our ServiceMonitors. Preflight could warn if ServiceMonitor selector doesn’t match.

Logan Wright: That’s hard to know. But we *can* validate our own objects: if they set `prometheus.serviceMonitor.labels` + `prometheus.prometheusRule.labels`, we can warn if labels are empty (operator defaults often require `release: prometheus` or similar).

Chloe Martin: Same for Grafana sidecar dashboards: warn if `grafana.sidecar.dashboards.label` doesn’t match what we apply.

Rafael Mendes: +1. The “dashboards empty” ticket almost always is label mismatch.

Connor O'Brien: So, concrete checks we implement in installer preflight (v1):
A) Permissions / API
- `kubectl auth can-i` checks for: create/update in namespace (ConfigMap, Secret, Service, Deployment, StatefulSet), and cluster-scoped if installing prom stack (CRD, ClusterRole, ClusterRoleBinding)
B) CRDs
- If `prometheus.external=true`: verify CRDs exist:
  - `servicemonitors.monitoring.coreos.com`
  - `podmonitors.monitoring.coreos.com` (optional?)
  - `prometheusrules.monitoring.coreos.com`
- If missing -> FAIL (unless `observability.exporterMode=annotations` is set)
C) Storage
- If deploying Prometheus/Grafana/Loki with persistence: verify a default StorageClass exists OR a StorageClass is explicitly set in values
- Attempt to create a tiny PVC as a canary? (create+delete)
D) Airgap / images
- If `global.imageRegistry` set: ensure imagePullSecret exists in target namespaces
- Optionally validate registry DNS resolve? (maybe too much)
E) Pod security / hostPath
- If logs collector DS uses hostPath: detect PSA restricted -> FAIL unless logs disabled
F) Value sanity
- warn on label mismatch risk (Grafana dashboard label, ServiceMonitor label)

Missing anything big?

Marcus Lin: On security: also FAIL if they try to enable request/response payload logging (if that’s even a flag) in restricted mode. But I think that’s out-of-scope for installer preflight.

Diego Santos: Another hard-blocker: if cluster doesn’t have enough allocatable resources for our requests and they’re installing the stack.
At least check nodes exist with GPU? (not for observability). But for prom/grafana we can sum requests:
- prom: 1-2 CPU, 4-8Gi
- grafana: 200m, 512Mi
- loki/collector: depends
Maybe WARN only. Scheduling depends on quotas/taints.

Rafael Mendes: I’d do WARN for “resources likely insufficient” and link sizing doc. Hard fail only if namespace ResourceQuota *already* makes it impossible (like max pods=0, or requests exceed hard limit). We can query quotas.

Sahana Iyer: We can implement a simple quota check:
- list ResourceQuota in namespace
- if hard limit exists for cpu/mem and our requested total > hard -> FAIL
Otherwise warn.

Dylan Brooks: + check for `LimitRange` min/max constraints. If they have min request > what we set, install fails later.

Connor O'Brien: Good call. Add LimitRange check.

Tomáš Novák: Re: UX: show output as:
- ✅ PASS
- ⚠️ WARN (continues)
- ❌ FAIL (stop)
And include copy-paste commands. Example:
```
FAIL: missing CRD prometheusrules.monitoring.coreos.com
Fix: install prometheus-operator CRDs (or set prometheus.external=false)
Runbook: https://confluence.redwood.ai/...
```

Chloe Martin: Also for Grafana: if they set `grafana.ingress.enabled=true` but don’t specify host/TLS secret, preflight WARN (or FAIL?)

Rafael Mendes: I’d WARN. Lots of customers do port-forward.

Allison Grant: Agreed. Don’t block unless it’s truly required.

Logan Wright: On CRDs: do we really need PodMonitor? I think we can do ServiceMonitor only. PodMonitor is nice.

Diego Santos: We’re using ServiceMonitor for most components. Runtime pods might be better as PodMonitor depending on how they expose ports, but we can ship both w/ toggle.

Connor O'Brien: Ok: ServiceMonitor + PrometheusRule required in external mode (FAIL if missing). PodMonitor optional (WARN if missing and `podMonitor.enabled=true`).

Marcus Lin: For PSA check, avoid false positives. If namespace enforce=restricted, we can WARN unless logs enabled *and* collector requires hostPath/privileged. Then FAIL.
