Sahana Iyer: +1. Also if they’re in OpenShift, SCC matters. We can’t reliably check SCC, but we can detect `openshift` API group and print guidance.

Dylan Brooks: Another preflight: existing resource conflicts.
If they already have a Grafana in the namespace and we try to create `grafana` service, we’ll collide.
We can check if key names exist (deploy/statefulset/service/configmap) and WARN/FAIL depending on `observability.manageStack`.

Connor O'Brien: Great point. Add:
- if `manageStack=true`: FAIL if existing resources with same names exist (prometheus/grafana/loki)
- if `manageStack=false`: skip

Rafael Mendes: This will save a lot of “half-installed” mess.

Chloe Martin: please include dashboards ConfigMap name check too. We’ve had “customer already has configmap grafana-dashboards” and we overwrite.

Connor O'Brien: ok.

Allison Grant: One more: time sync. Prometheus is sensitive to huge clock drift (alerts weird). Can we check node time? Probably not. Mention in postinstall.

Tomáš Novák: Keep it simple.

Connor O'Brien: Summarizing decisions:
FAIL (stop install):
- missing required RBAC perms (based on mode)
- missing required CRDs for external Prometheus (ServiceMonitor, PrometheusRule)
- persistence enabled but no StorageClass / PVC cannot be created
- logs collector requires hostPath/privileged but namespace PSA restricted (unless logs disabled)
- resource quota / limitrange makes scheduling impossible
- conflicting existing resources when manageStack=true

WARN (continue):
- ingress enabled but no controller/host/tls configured
- label mismatch risks (grafana sidecar / servicemonitor)
- podmonitor CRD missing when podmonitor enabled
- default deny networkpolicy detected w/out allow hints
- sizing below recs

I’ll bake this into the preflight spec + PR checklist. If you have edge cases, drop them here.

Rafael Mendes: Looks good. :thumbsup:

Diego Santos: +1. I’ll add notes about PVC canary create/delete and conflict detection.

Sahana Iyer: I can wire a couple of these into smoke tests too (e.g., fail when ServiceMonitor CRD missing in external mode) so we don’t regress.

Chloe Martin: I’ll DM Connor a list of the exact grafana sidecar labels we use in the dashboards bundle so preflight messaging is accurate.

Marcus Lin: + please ensure preflight output doesn’t dump secrets (like image pull secret contents) when it fails.

Connor O'Brien: yup, only names/paths. Thanks all.

Caleb Johnson: quick q on ENG-7732 (signal aggregation job) — are we committing to daily windows only for MVP or trying to do hourly + daily rollups? I’m worried about telemetry latency + late arriving events. If we do daily only, what’s the expected lag before HubSpot tasks fire?

Mei Lin: I’d strongly prefer daily for v1. Hourly adds a lot of state + retries + cost, and we’ll end up reprocessing anyway.

Mei Lin: For “how fast do we need to alert Sales” — I think within same day is fine. Like: if a customer is spiking today, they’re still spiking tomorrow morning.

Logan Wright: +1 daily for MVP. We can still *run the daily job multiple times/day* if needed (same window) w/ idempotency.

Caleb Johnson: got it. but we’ve been saying “429 storm” should trigger follow-up quickly. waiting 24h feels long.

Logan Wright: yeah. we can meet in the middle: daily windows but compute with a watermark and rerun.

Logan Wright: proposal:
- canonical aggregation window = UTC day (00:00–23:59)
- job runs every hour, but it only recomputes “today” + “yesterday” (or last 2 days) to catch late events
- it writes to the same partition keyed by (org_id, signal_type, window_start) so reruns are upserts

Logan Wright: so you get near-real-time-ish without having *hourly* windows that explode the signal volume.

Mei Lin: :thumbsup: this is basically “hourly compute / daily semantics”

Caleb Johnson: makes sense. what do we assume for event latency? I’ve seen ingestion delayed 20-40m during peak.

Nadia Rahman: jumping in — telemetry pipeline typical lag is 5–15m, but p95 can be ~45m when we’re backlogging. Also some events (billing reconciliation) can show up hours later.

Nadia Rahman: if the signal is based on request logs (tokens, 429s, latency), it’s mostly within the hour. if it’s based on billing/spend, expect longer.

Priya Natarajan: +1 to keep v1 simple. “hourly windowing” sounds like a whole other product.

Priya Natarajan: We should be explicit in the PRD/ADR: outcome latency target for routing tasks is *same business day* not “instant.”

Caleb Johnson: cool. for backfills, are we expecting manual runs? or an automated “last N days recompute”?

Mei Lin: both. automated small backfill each run + manual for incidents.

Mei Lin: suggested:
- every hourly run recompute last 2 days (today/yesterday)
- nightly run recompute last 7 days (cheap-ish) to mop up stragglers
- manual CLI supports `--start_date` / `--end_date` for one-off backfills

Logan Wright: + add an “ingestion_complete_at” watermark so we can measure how much we’re shifting due to late data.

Logan Wright: also we should store `computed_at` and `job_run_id` for audit. HubSpot folks will ask “why did this trigger now?”

Caleb Johnson: +1. where are we documenting the window choice? ADR schema v1?

Logan Wright: ADR (usage/intent signals schema v1) covers window fields but not cadence. We should add a short addendum in ENG-7732 description + comment in the PR.

Logan Wright: pinging reviewers: @Mei Lin @Nadia Rahman @Priya Natarajan can you sanity check the above proposal? want to avoid us changing semantics mid-implementation.

Cole Summers: from billing side: please don’t mix “usage” + “spend” in the same latency expectations.

Cole Summers: If signal is “spend velocity,” we’ll be deriving it from metered usage anyway, but invoices/credits adjustments can land later. I’d treat spend velocity as “daily accurate,” not “hourly accurate.”

Mei Lin: agree. we can label signal sources:
- request telemetry signals (429_rate, token_volume) = eligible for hourly compute / fast routing
- billing-derived signals (spend_velocity) = daily compute / route next day

Caleb Johnson: ok. for the table schema, I’m thinking something like:
```
window_start (date)
window_end (date)
org_id
workspace_id (optional?)
signal_type
metric_value
sample_count
confidence
computed_at
backfill_reason
```

Caleb Johnson: (workspace_id optional b/c some of the “team expansion” signals are workspace-scoped)

Mei Lin: careful w/ workspace_id — we said we route at company/org level to HubSpot. if we include workspace_id it should be for debugging, not part of the primary key.

Logan Wright: +1. key should be stable and dedupe-friendly:
- `dedupe_key = org_id + signal_type + window_start`
- we can store `top_workspace_ids` as a string list later if needed, but don’t block MVP.

Priya Natarajan: also: choose “window_end exclusive” semantics if we ever move to timestamps. For daily dates it’s fine but let’s not set a trap.

Nadia Rahman: re latency SLO: can we set a formal target?
- telemetry-based signals: <2h after threshold crossing (given hourly compute)
- billing-based signals: next day by 10am local

Logan Wright: works for me. lets capture that in ENG-7732 + monitoring ticket (ENG-7824) so we alert if lag blows up.

Caleb Johnson: ack. I’ll update ENG-7732 w/:
- daily window semantics (UTC)
- job runs hourly, recomputes today + yesterday
- nightly 7d mop-up
- manual backfill flags

Caleb Johnson: also will add metrics:
- `aggregation_lag_seconds`
- `late_event_rate`

Mei Lin: :thumbsup: and please add a note about timezones (UTC only). we’ve been burned.

Logan Wright: + if anyone objects, speak now :wink: otherwise I’ll reference this thread in PR-28458.

Priya Natarajan: no objection. ship it.

Nadia Rahman: good w/ me.

Cole Summers: fine as long as spend velocity isn’t used for “same day” promises.

Caleb Johnson: cool, thanks all.

Ethan Park: heads up for anyone tracking the model registry migration — we need to lock timing for the metadata v1 backfill + staging validation before we cut over in prod. don’t want a repeat of “silent” model changes showing up w/o release notes.

Michael Nguyen: yup. PR-18429 (migration + backfill) is ready behind the feature flag. current plan was: run backfill job in staging, validate invariants + changelog generation, then schedule prod window.

Talia Benson: can we get concrete dates? i’m trying to line up comms w/ Support + release train.

Connor O'Brien: +1. also reminder: anything touching release-note preview CI needs to land before Thursday’s train or we’re in the awkward half-state.

Quentin Harris: for backfill ownership: my team can provide the mapping table + default values for older model records (esp quantization profiles). but i don’t want catalog owning the actual run in prod.

Michael Nguyen: agree. I can own execution + monitoring of the backfill. Quentin: please sanity check the defaults we’re applying for `compatibility` + `tokenizer` fields.

Sahana Iyer: fwiw validation in CI is already catching missing required fields in fixtures, but staging is where we’ll see “real” bad records. we should define the staging gate criteria (no schema violations, no redaction leaks in generated notes, etc.)

Jada Williams: from console side: as long as the registry records have stable IDs + version strings after backfill, we can render change history. biggest risk for us is if we end up renaming versions during migration and break pinned references.

Diego Santos: and for Private/Dedicated: we can’t assume all deployments get the same rollout day. any “availability” fields in metadata v1 must not get auto-populated incorrectly. i’d like us to validate at least 1 private bundle build in staging.

Chloe Martin: +1 on pinned versions. we have support escalations tied to “what exact model+variant did customer run on date X”. if we change identifiers, the UI/history becomes useless.

Ethan Park: ok let’s break down:
1) WHO runs the backfill?
2) WHEN do we run staging?
3) WHAT are gates to proceed to prod?
4) WHEN do we do prod cutover?

Michael Nguyen: 1) I’ll run. I’ll also prep rollback (revert schema version marker + restore pre-backfill snapshot). For 2) I can run staging tomorrow morning PT.

Connor O'Brien: tomorrow morning works. if staging gate passes by EOD tomorrow, we can target prod Tuesday 10am PT (outside peak). anything later and we collide with end-of-week train.

Talia Benson: Tuesday 10am PT is good — i’ll ping Support/CS with heads-up of “possible model catalog metadata updates” but will emphasize no customer-visible behavior change intended.

Quentin Harris: re: defaults — main open question is risk classification. older records don’t have clear “breaking vs non-breaking” notes. we should default to “unknown/needs review” rather than “low risk”.

Sahana Iyer: yes please. for the schema we can set `risk_level: unspecified` (or whatever the enum is) and have generator template render a neutral line instead of claiming safe.

Michael Nguyen: current backfill draft sets `risk_level=low` unless we detect tokenizer/runtime changes. I can change that to `unspecified` and only set `low` when we have explicit prior signals.

Ethan Park: do that. we should be conservative.

Jada Williams: also for “public vs internal”: the backfill job shouldn’t copy internal notes into public fields. we already had that console bug last week (internal notes surfaced to non-admins). can we add a staging check that generated public notes don’t contain any `internal_*` markers?

Sahana Iyer: i can add a quick grep-based smoke check for staging artifacts. but ideally we validate via the generator’s redaction rules (unit test + sample output).

Diego Santos: staging gate should include: generate release note preview for 3 cases: Hosted-only model, Dedicated w/ region constraints, Private model that’s “available: false” on Hosted. make sure output matches.

Connor O'Brien: what about timing of event emission? if we backfill a bunch of records, does it emit “model metadata changed” events and spam the changelog feed?

Michael Nguyen: good catch. backfill is doing writes w/ `suppress_public_changelog=true` flag, but still emits internal audit events. public release notes should only be generated on promotion events, not backfill.

Chloe Martin: audit events are fine. support wants those.

Ethan Park: make sure we can prove in staging that we didn’t accidentally publish 500 “new release notes” pages.

Michael Nguyen: yep. staging run plan:
- run migration
- run backfill in batches (500 records)
- validate: schema violations = 0, pinned version IDs unchanged, sample models have correct availability + compat constraints
- validate: changelog feed count unchanged (or within expected)
- validate: audit events emitted

Talia Benson: who’s doing the validation sign-off? i need names for the checklist.

Ethan Park: Proposed owners:
- Michael: run + DB metrics + rollback readiness
- Quentin: spot-check catalog field correctness (compat/tokenizer/quant profiles)
- Jada/Chloe: console read-path sanity (change history endpoint doesn’t break)
- Diego: private bundle build / offline export sanity
- Connor: release train + go/no-go for Tuesday
- Sahana: schema validation + redaction checks

Connor O'Brien: works.

Quentin Harris: ok. i can be online during staging run and will review a sample set (top 10 models by traffic + a few legacy ones).

Diego Santos: i can run a private bundle build in staging env if i can get the artifact pipeline pointed at the staging registry. michael, send me the staging endpoint + credentials path.

Michael Nguyen: will do.

DeployBot: Reminder: change freeze for production begins at 2025-04-03 18:00 PT for quarterly infra maintenance. Coordinate releases accordingly.

Connor O'Brien: ugh, ok, that means Tuesday is safest.

Sahana Iyer: one more: do we need to backfill “deprecation” fields? a bunch of legacy models are effectively deprecated but not flagged.

Quentin Harris: not in this first pass. we’ll only backfill required fields + safe defaults. deprecation timelines need PM/Docs alignment.

Ethan Park: agree. keep it minimal.

Chloe Martin: can we also capture a snapshot of “before/after” for 1-2 models so Support has an example of how change history will look?

Talia Benson: i’ll ask Dev Patel what format they want, but a screenshot + a link to internal admin view should be enough.

Ethan Park: action items:
- Michael: update backfill defaults (risk_level -> unspecified unless explicit), run staging tomorrow 9am PT, post results + metrics here.
- Quentin: be on standby tomorrow 9–11am PT for sample validation.
- Diego: run private bundle build after backfill completes; report any availability/packaging issues.
- Sahana: add staging smoke check for redaction + changelog spam (public feed delta).
- Connor/Talia: tentatively hold prod cutover Tuesday 10am PT pending staging gate.

Michael Nguyen: ack. i’ll post the staging checklist doc + query snippets once i finish updating the job.

Connor O'Brien: please include rollback triggers (e.g., >0.5% schema validation failures, change in pinned version resolution, unexpected public release notes generated) so we can make the call fast.

Ethan Park: +1. let’s treat this like a mini-migration with explicit abort criteria.

Talia Benson: hey folks, lining up cross-team reviews for SIEM-ready audit export beta work. We’re trying to get these into next week’s release train + start internal alpha.

Links (please add :eyes: when you start review, :thumbsup: when approved):
1) Exporter: metrics/tracing — https://github.com/redwoodinference/audit-log-exporter/pull/1098
2) Exporter: egress allowlist + destination validation — https://github.com/redwoodinference/audit-log-exporter/pull/1105
3) Exporter: integration tests (splunk/datadog sandboxes) — https://github.com/redwoodinference/audit-log-exporter/pull/1112
4) Exporter: backpressure + max lag auto-pause — https://github.com/redwoodinference/audit-log-exporter/pull/1119
5) Control plane: export config CRUD endpoints — https://github.com/redwoodinference/redwood/pull/28766
6) Control plane: RBAC perms — https://github.com/redwoodinference/redwood/pull/28792
7) Control plane: status endpoint used by Console — https://github.com/redwoodinference/redwood/pull/28805
8) Control plane: feature flag + tenant allowlist — https://github.com/redwoodinference/redwood/pull/28824

Reviewer asks (trying to avoid drive-by reviews):
- Security: secrets handling, egress/SSRF, log redaction, least-priv RBAC
- SRE: SLO signals, paging vs ticket semantics, auto-pause behavior
- Observability: metric naming/labels + dashboard compatibility
- Console: status endpoint contract + UX implications (error messaging)

If you’re slammed, just reply w/ who to pull in + when you can take it. 🙏

Ethan Park: ack. I can own the control plane ones (28766/28805/28824) as primary reviewer. Benji can be backup.

Benji Okafor: yep, I’ll take first pass on 28766 validation bits + storage/migration notes. :eyes:

Kaitlyn Nguyen: I’ll review 28792 (RBAC) + also scan 28766 for permission checks around create/update/delete config + credential rotation. :eyes:

Marcus Lin: I’ll do security pass on exporter PRs 1105 + 1119 (egress + backpressure/auto-pause failure modes). Pls tag me explicitly if there’s any new logging of request payloads.

Hanae Suzuki: can review anything touching secrets/KMS/credential refs. which PR includes secret retrieval path? (I see 1105 mentions validation only?)

Talia Benson: Hanae — on this set it’s mostly “secret refs” and destination validation. Secret retrieval is in the earlier exporter scaffold/config sync, but 1105 adds some new checks around host allowlist. Still would love your eyes on: 
- 28766 request schema (how we reference secrets)
- 1105 (ensuring we don’t leak secret values in errors/logs)

Logan Wright: I’ll take 1098 (metrics) as primary + coordinate with Caleb on label cardinality. :eyes:

Caleb Johnson: +1. will specifically check metric naming conventions + labels (tenant_id gating) + any high-cardinality tags. :eyes:

Allison Grant: I can do an SRE-ish review on 1098/1119 from the alerting angle (what signals will be reliable). Not full code deep dive but will leave comments on semantics.

Sean Gallagher: I’ll review 1119 (max lag + auto-pause) for operational behavior. Particularly: does it “pause” per destination vs per tenant vs global? and what does it do to lag metrics.

Rafael Mendes: I can cover 1112 integration tests (are they stable, can we run in CI without flake). Also quick look at 1098 for anything that impacts oncall dashboards.

Dylan Brooks: can someone from Console/Control plane confirm if 28805 response shape is final? We need to wire it to the settings page and the error messages are going to be customer-visible.

Chloe Martin: I’ll review 28805 contract + cache behavior (tenancy checks / auth). :eyes:

Connor O'Brien: release note: if we want these next train, approvals need to land by EOD Thu. also reminder: 28824 (tenant allowlist) needs to be merged before we turn on beta flag in prod.

Ethan Park: on 28805: contract should be stable for beta but we may add fields later (non-breaking). we’ll commit to keeping existing keys.

Marcus Lin: quick ask for 1105 reviewers: make sure destination validation includes scheme/port restrictions. no arbitrary URLs; only known hostnames for splunk/datadog + S3 is not “hostname” in same way.

Logan Wright: on 1098: please don’t put raw destination URL or bucket name as metric label. fine in logs (redacted) but not in metrics.

Sean Gallagher: +1. Also ensure we can page on processing lag not just “event timestamp lag” during backfills, otherwise we’ll light ourselves on fire.

Talia Benson: noted. Sean — that aligns w/ the SLO thread you all had (processing vs pipeline lag). If 1098 doesn’t have both, flag it and we’ll fix before merge.

Hanae Suzuki: ok I’ll start w/ 28766 + 1105 tonight. main things I’ll look for:
- errors don’t echo secret refs or tokens
- secret refs are opaque IDs (no inline creds)
- audit trail doesn’t store sensitive config blobs

Kaitlyn Nguyen: for RBAC: pls ensure “view status” is separate from “manage destinations”. we don’t want auditors being able to rotate tokens.

Benji Okafor: 28766 uses separate perms for read vs write, but I’ll double-check the “test connection” flow isn’t accidentally a write path.

Chloe Martin: FYI console will call status endpoint pretty frequently (polling). 28805 should be cached + cheap, otherwise we’ll DoS ourselves.

Ethan Park: yep 28805 has a short TTL cache in the service layer; I’ll make sure it’s keyed by tenant+destination and respects auth.

Rafael Mendes: for 1112: if these spin up actual Splunk, can we ensure it uses stubs in CI? last time we had a dockerized vendor service it was super flaky.

Talia Benson: 1112 is supposed to use a lightweight stub/harness (not full splunk) — but please confirm. if it’s heavy, we’ll gate to nightly.

Connor O'Brien: +1, if it’s flaky we’ll move it out of required checks. but for beta we need at least one green e2e run per release.

Allison Grant: also, request: in 1119 please document what triggers auto-pause and how to unpause (manual vs automatic). this is going straight into runbook.

Sean Gallagher: yep. if it’s “auto resume” after cooldown, call that out. if it’s “requires operator”, also call out.

Marcus Lin: I’ll add comments in 1119 if the pause/unpause state could be abused (e.g., attacker forces pause to blind SIEM). maybe needs audit events for “export paused” state transitions.

Ethan Park: good point. we can emit an audit event for config changes + state changes (pause/resume) in control plane; for exporter-internal pause maybe a control plane status update. will see what’s already in 28805/28766.

Talia Benson: awesome. I’ll track approvals in the beta readiness sheet. please drop a quick comment here if any PR is “blocker” so we can reshuffle.

Logan Wright: started 1098 review. :eyes:

Sean Gallagher: started 1119 review. :eyes:

Kaitlyn Nguyen: started 28792 review. :eyes:

Chloe Martin: started 28805 review. :eyes:

Rafael Mendes: starting 1112 review now. :eyes:

Hanae Suzuki: starting 28766 then 1105. :eyes:

Connor O'Brien: Heads up: we’re trying to lock docs for Dedicated throughput SLO + tiers before Sales Enablement training next week. Need cross-team review/sign-off on the Confluence set by EOD Thu so we don’t ship conflicting language.

Connor O'Brien: Links (please comment inline in Confluence):
- PRD (Product): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/dedicated-throughput-slo-prd
- SLO definition (SRE): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/dedicated-throughput-slo-definition
- ADR-027 metric decision (Arch): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/adr-027-throughput-slo
- Pricing/packaging v1 (Prod+Fin): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/dedicated-pricing-tiers-v1
- Sales cheat sheet draft: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/dedicated-tier-cheat-sheet

Connor O'Brien: Specific asks:
1) confirm canonical metric + exclusions are consistent across PRD/SLO/cheat sheet
2) tier entitlements language (burst/failover/support response) is consistent
3) pricing doc has guardrails that match Deal Desk expectations

Lauren Bishop: +1. Also pls sanity check the customer-facing phrasing in PRD section “SLO wording” — we keep getting asked “is this in writing?” and we need one answer per tier.

Sean Gallagher: I’ll review the SLO definition + ADR today. One thing: make sure the streaming accounting note is updated post PR-19422 (double counting fix). We had some older screenshots in the doc.

Rafael Mendes: Can we align on whether we’re calling it “tokens/sec” or “output tokens/sec”? We’ve had confusion in oncall. The evaluator uses generated tokens + prompt tokens depending on route.

Allison Grant: +1 to Rafael. From Observability POV, the dashboards show both “total tokens/sec” and “generated tokens/sec”. If customer-facing says one, internal should match.

Lauren Bishop: For customer-facing commitment, I’d strongly prefer “generated output tokens/sec” (floor) and treat prompt processing as part of latency SLO (not in scope here). But if SRE says measurement reality is total, we need to reconcile.

Sean Gallagher: Measurement reality right now is “billable tokens/sec” (prompt + generated) because it’s what we can reliably tag by deployment_id, and it tracks capacity consumption. ADR-027 explicitly chose that to avoid gaming via tiny outputs.

Rafael Mendes: Yep. Also if we promise output-only, someone can slam huge prompts and claim we missed even though GPUs are doing work. That becomes an exclusion nightmare.

Elliot Price: If the promise is billable tokens/sec, we need to be explicit in docs. Right now the PRD uses “tokens/sec” in some places and “generated tokens/sec” in others. I’ll add a glossary box.

Kira Thompson: I can take the pass to harmonize terminology across PRD + cheat sheet + FAQs. Please @me on the preferred final wording. Suggest we define:
- “Throughput” = billable tokens/sec measured at the deployment
- “Billable tokens” = input + output after server-side normalization (streaming safe)

Sergio Costa: For the dashboard spec doc, I’ll update labels to match: “Dedicated Throughput (billable tok/s)”. Also will add a tooltip explaining composition.

Irene Choi: I’ll review the shape catalog references in PRD + pricing. I saw the PRD mention L40S as a default in eu-west which isn’t true for current supply. We should avoid listing specific GPUs in customer-facing docs unless we can guarantee.

Vanessa Ortiz: +1. Shapes are stable but underlying GPU type might vary by region. We can say “Shape D2 (2x GPU)” instead of “2x H100” publicly.

Lauren Bishop: Agree. Tiers should map to shapes; shapes map to regions; GPU specifics can be in internal appendix only.

Laura Bennett: I’ll review pricing/packaging v1 + cheat sheet guardrails today. Reminder: we said no discounts below floor without Deal Desk approval and finance sign-off (esp Premium). Please ensure the cheat sheet reflects the triggers.

Connor O'Brien: Thanks. Can we set owners for inline comments?
- SLO/ADR: @Sean @Rafael
- Shapes references: @Irene @Vanessa
- Pricing/guardrails: @Laura
- Terminology + customer-facing wording pass: @Elliot @Kira
- Dashboards language alignment: @Allison @Sergio

Allison Grant: Works. Also: please ensure the SLO doc points to the correct dashboard panel IDs. The current link in SLO definition goes to the old “throughput-exploration” board.

Sergio Costa: Yep, will fix. New board is “Dedicated / SLO Compliance” in Grafana. I’ll update links and add a “how to interpret” section.

Mina Haddad: Quick note: tier-aware routing doc should be referenced (for internal). Sales keeps asking “what happens under contention?” and the cheat sheet hints at “priority routing” but doesn’t define what that means.

Lauren Bishop: Good call. For customer-facing, we should say “traffic is isolated within dedicated capacity; in rare cases we may apply admission control to preserve committed throughput.” Internal doc can explain the actual shedding rules.

Rafael Mendes: Please be careful: “isolated” can be read as “never impacted.” We’ve got failure modes (node drain, model reload, warm pool). We need a clean exclusions paragraph.

Sean Gallagher: The SLO doc already has exclusions, but PRD summary doesn’t. I’ll paste a shorter version into PRD + cheat sheet:
- customer-side throttling / client timeouts
- unsupported models or model versions not pinned
- planned maintenance windows (if notified)
- warmup after deploy/scale events (first N minutes)
- abuse patterns (sustained >burst cap)

Elliot Price: On the “warm pool behavior”: do we have a concrete number? The docs currently say “brief warmup.” That’s vague.

Irene Choi: Internally: 10 min warmup budget is what we’ve been using for new pools (depends on model size). For customer docs, we can say “up to 15 minutes after provisioning or model change” and treat it as excluded.

Rafael Mendes: Make sure that exclusion doesn’t swallow the SLO. If we do 15 min exclusions repeatedly, it’s basically not a guarantee. Maybe phrase as “after explicit customer-initiated model change or Redwood-initiated maintenance event.”

Lauren Bishop: Agree. We should keep exclusions tight.

Connor O'Brien: Action items:
- Everyone: please add inline comments by Thu 5pm PT.
- @Kira/@Elliot: consolidate wording + push suggested diffs in one comment thread per doc (so we don’t miss things).
- @Sean/@Rafael: final answer on metric naming + measurement window (is it 5m rolling? 30m?) so Sales isn’t improvising.

Sean Gallagher: Measurement window in SLO doc is 30-min rolling for compliance, with 5-min for fast burn alerts. I’ll add an FAQ line: “We measure compliance over 30 minutes; we alert internally on shorter windows.”

Laura Bennett: Pricing doc has one open question: whether Plus includes “burst included” or “burst billable”. Finance prefers burst billable (metered) to protect GM. PRD currently implies included burst.

Lauren Bishop: For Plus we said “burst available at metered rate up to X%” (not included). Premium gets a small included burst pool (as a concession). I’ll fix PRD language if it reads included for Plus.

Sergio Costa: If Premium has included burst, I need to ensure metering dashboard differentiates committed vs included burst vs paid burst. Otherwise CS will have a bad time.

Allison Grant: The metering events from PR-19380 have “commit”, “burst_paid”. Do we have “burst_included” yet?

Mina Haddad: I don’t think so. That might be a follow-up.

Connor O'Brien: Let’s not block this doc pass on a missing label; just add a note: “included burst tracking will appear in usage exports starting vNext” and confirm with @Nadia/@Cole later.

Kira Thompson: I’ll add a callout in cheat sheet: “For Premium, a limited amount of burst may be included (see Order Form). Usage exports may show as burst until vNext adds included/billed split.”

Rafael Mendes: Re: enablement: do we have a hard date for internal preview vs external? Don’t want people sending the Confluence link to customers.

Lauren Bishop: We’re treating these Confluence pages as internal. Customer-facing version will be in redwood-docs (public site) after Legal review.

Elliot Price: I’ll add an “Internal Only” banner to the Confluence pages + a note that external language lives in docs PR (redwood-docs/pr-1442).

Connor O'Brien: Perfect. Please react with :eyes: when you’ve reviewed your sections and :white_check_mark: when done.

Sean Gallagher: :eyes:

Irene Choi: :eyes:

Laura Bennett: :eyes:

Sergio Costa: :eyes:

Allison Grant: :eyes:

Kira Thompson: :eyes:

Elliot Price: :eyes:

Rafael Mendes: Quick last thing: can we add a one-liner in the cheat sheet for escalation path? Support keeps getting looped without a playbook.

Lauren Bishop: Yes, link the escalation playbook (internal) and a simple promise: “Premium: 24/7 paging, 30-min response target; Plus: business-hours + best effort; Standard: next business day.” (matching tier definitions).

Connor O'Brien: Great. Let’s land all edits Thu and I’ll freeze + hand to Ava for enablement packaging.

Vanessa Ortiz: :white_check_mark: left comments on shape language + removed hard GPU claims.

Sean Gallagher: :white_check_mark: updated SLO doc to consistently say “billable tok/s”, added measurement window + streaming accounting note.

Laura Bennett: :white_check_mark: added discount floor + deal desk triggers; flagged Plus burst language as metered.

Kira Thompson: :white_check_mark: harmonized terminology in PRD + cheat sheet; added “Internal Only” banner suggestion.

Sergio Costa: :white_check_mark: updated dashboard spec links + panel naming.

Connor O'Brien: Thanks all. I’ll do a final skim tomorrow morning and then lock for enablement.

source.thread:
Benji Okafor: sharing early permission inventory coverage report (from ENG-18241). this is *not* final but enough to start assigning owners + closing gaps before we flip deny-by-default more broadly.

Doc: <https://drive.redwoodinference.com/engineering/platform-engineering/permissions-inventory-endpoint-audit> (tab: "Coverage 02/16")

High level numbers (hosted+control-plane+console API):
- 312 endpoints + background jobs sampled
- 247 mapped to a permission string (79%)
- 38 endpoints missing *any* authz check (12%)
- 27 endpoints have authz but inconsistent semantics (8%) (wrong 401/403, missing `X-Redwood-Deny-Reason`, etc)

Biggest buckets of missing checks:
1) streaming/chat routes (gateway) - a couple paths bypass the new middleware
2) control plane: org/user/role mutations have checks, but a few "read" endpoints return sensitive info (keys metadata, audit retention) without explicit perm
3) console admin actions: role assignment flows calling internal endpoints that aren't annotated

Top offenders list (first pass):
```
[Gateway]
- POST /v1/chat/completions:stream (missing permission check on legacy handler)
- POST /v1/text/generate:stream (missing check)

[Control Plane]
- GET /orgs/{orgId}/api-keys (returns key metadata; currently only authn)
- POST /orgs/{orgId}/routing-policies/{policyId}:validate (should be same perm as update? unclear)
- POST /orgs/{orgId}/secrets/{name}:rotate (check exists in svc but not enforced at gateway)

[Audit/Export]
- POST /orgs/{orgId}/audit-logs:export (has check but maps to old coarse role; no v2 perm yet)

[Console Backend]
- POST /orgs/{orgId}/role-bindings (missing permission annotation, reachable from UI)
- DELETE /orgs/{orgId}/roles/{roleId} (annotated but not covered by e2e test; saw 500 vs 403 mismatch)
```

Asks:
- please reply on the endpoints you can own + ETA for fix/PR
- if you disagree on mapping (esp validate vs update), call it out here; Jordan/Kaitlyn can help with naming + scope
- Leila: once we agree on the list, can we add these to the "critical permissions" integration test suite?

Goal for this week: bring missing-check count from 38 -> <10 and lock down the streaming bypasses.

CoverageBot: :bar_chart: Coverage snapshot uploaded (commit 8b7c1f2). Missing checks: 38. Inconsistent semantics: 27. New since last snapshot: +5 endpoints, +2 missing checks.

Amara Diallo: looking at the gateway ones now. i think both streaming routes are hitting the legacy mux, not the new middleware chain. should be straightforward.

Amara Diallo: i can take:
- POST /v1/chat/completions:stream
- POST /v1/text/generate:stream
ETA: PR tomorrow morning.

Marcus Lin: +1 priority on streaming bypass. we should treat those as sev2 if confirmed. please add regression tests.

Ethan Park: i’ll grab the control plane reads.
- GET /orgs/{orgId}/api-keys (agree this needs explicit permission even though it’s "metadata")
- POST /orgs/{orgId}/routing-policies/{policyId}:validate (need product decision: same perm as update vs separate `routing_policy.validate`)

Ethan Park: can land annotations + checks for api-keys list by EOW. for validate i’ll wait for Jordan/Naomi guidance.

Jordan Lee: on validate: from a least-privilege perspective, separate perm is nice, but it explodes matrix. pragmatically i'd map to same as update for v2, then split later if needed.

Jordan Lee: proposed mapping:
- `redwood.routing_policies.update` (covers validate + update + dry-run)
- if later needed: `...validate` can be introduced as v3 permission with backwards compat.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: +1 to Jordan. also note: validate endpoint currently returns expanded policy evaluation output (includes cluster names + fallbacks) so it’s not "harmless". using update perm is defensible.

Diego Santos: also for Dedicated/Private, validate sometimes reaches into cluster-scoped config. so please don’t make it more permissive than update.

Benji Okafor: cool, i’ll mark validate->update mapping in the sheet unless Naomi objects.

Chloe Martin: i’ll take the console backend endpoints.
- POST /orgs/{orgId}/role-bindings (missing annotation)
- DELETE /orgs/{orgId}/roles/{roleId} (semantics mismatch)

Chloe Martin: ETA: by Wed. i’ll also add a unit test asserting all role mgmt endpoints have permission annotations.

Felix Schneider: can help on the "why denied" / debug headers side if we touch the console flows. i saw a couple places UI assumes 401 and retries auth; should be 403 with reason.

Kevin Osei: for audit export mapping: we need a v2 perm string for export. i suggest `redwood.audit_logs.export` scoped to org + maybe project? (but export is org-wide today).

Kevin Osei: i can draft mapping + update exporter side once perm is in registry. but someone (Jordan?) needs to bless naming/versioning.

Jordan Lee: naming looks fine. per ADR-014 we should keep it plural + verb. `redwood.audit_logs.export` works. add to registry with sensitivity tier "high".

Brian Murphy: can we ensure export also requires `audit_logs.read`? or is export the superset? asking because some customers want "view in console" but not bulk export.

Marcus Lin: export should be separate + stronger. don’t imply read->export.

Jordan Lee: agree. define both:
- `redwood.audit_logs.read`
- `redwood.audit_logs.export`

Benji Okafor: noted, i’ll add both to the inventory + flag for role matrix update.

Leila Farouk: once Amara/Chloe/Ethan PRs are in, i can add e2e coverage for:
- streaming endpoints require perm
- role binding create/delete
- api key list + rotate
- audit export

Leila Farouk: pls tag me on PRs so i can grab exact routes + expected deny reason codes.

Connor O'Brien: rollout note: if we’re fixing streaming middleware, please coordinate with me before merge so we don’t accidentally change latency/headers in the middle of the pilot. feature flag gating still applies.

Amara Diallo: will do. i’ll keep it behind the existing `rbac_v2_gateway_enforce` flag and only apply to the legacy mux handlers.

Benji Okafor: thanks all. action items:
- Amara: streaming fixes PR by tomorrow, add regression test hooks
- Ethan: api-keys list check + annotation by EOW; validate mapped to update perm
- Chloe: role binding/role delete annotations + 403 semantics by Wed
- Kevin/Jordan: add `audit_logs.read` + `audit_logs.export` to registry + update mapping
- Leila: add to critical permissions e2e suite after merges

Benji Okafor: i’ll update the coverage sheet tonight + repost counts. :thumbsup:

source.content:
Kira Thompson: Dropped a draft of the Private upgrade runbook steps in Confluence (WIP). Before I lock wording, can folks sanity check the “verification” section? Specifically what commands we want operators to run + what we consider “upgrade success”.

Rafael Mendes: +1. The current draft says “verify system healthy” but that’s vague. We should be explicit about control plane vs data plane, and what “green” means.

Connor O'Brien: Can you link the exact section? Also we need to ensure it matches what installer actually prints now (plan/apply/rollback commands).

Kira Thompson: Section is “Post-upgrade verification”. Right now it has:
- `kubectl get pods`
- “check console loads”
- “check logs for errors”

Sean Gallagher: That’s too generic. For on-call we want deterministic checks + thresholds. Also we should call out what to do if a check fails (rollback vs wait vs page).

Ethan Park: From CP side: success is not “pods running”, it’s “CP reachable + reconciler stable + DB migrations completed”. Can we add a CP health endpoint check (whatever installer uses)?

Irene Choi: Infra add: storage + node pressure should be part of “done”. We’ve seen upgrades succeed but cluster is in DiskPressure and then it falls over later.

Sahana Iyer: For CI we’re using a canonical sequence. We can mirror that. Something like:
1) `redwood-private status --output=json` (or `--verbose`) and ensure it reports target version + “Ready”
2) verify helm releases are at expected chart versions
3) verify migrations job completed
4) verify core request path (smoke test)

Dev Patel: + please make it support-friendly. When Support asks “did it succeed?”, customers need a checklist they can paste back with outputs. If we can standardize the commands, we can have support bundle parse them later.

Owen Phillips: Yeah. Also define “acceptable warnings”. Some customers will see transient 5xx during rollout; the runbook should say when that’s acceptable vs an incident.

Kira Thompson: OK. What exact commands do we want? I can write them but need the source of truth.

Connor O'Brien: For helm, we usually do:
- `helm list -n redwood-system`
- `helm history <release> -n redwood-system | head`
And verify the revision incremented + status is `deployed`.
We should also include `kubectl get events -n redwood-system --sort-by=.lastTimestamp | tail -n 30`.

Rafael Mendes: For “upgrade success” I’d define as ALL:
- installer state says finished (no pending steps)
- control plane healthy (readyz + reconciler loops)
- data plane rollouts complete (all DP deployments available)
- migrations complete (no pending/failed)
- smoke test passes (a small inference call)
- no P1 alerts firing for 30 min (or within agreed window)

Sean Gallagher: +1 but be careful with “30 min” as a hard requirement (some customers won’t want to wait). Maybe “monitor for at least 10–15 min after completion; 30 min recommended for large clusters”.

Ethan Park: For migrations, we need a concrete check. If we have a k8s job like `redwood-migrations` we can do:
`kubectl get jobs -n redwood-system | grep migrat`
and then `kubectl logs job/<jobname> -n redwood-system --tail=200`.
Also check DB lock contention if job runs >N minutes.

Irene Choi: Cluster pressure check I’d add:
- `kubectl get nodes`
- `kubectl describe node <node> | egrep -i 'DiskPressure|MemoryPressure|PIDPressure'`
and
- `kubectl top nodes` (if metrics-server is present)
But might be optional since not all on-prem has metrics.

Sahana Iyer: For the installer, what we rely on in tests:
- `redwood-private upgrade plan --from <current> --to <target> --output plan.json` (pre)
- `redwood-private upgrade apply --plan plan.json`
Post:
- `redwood-private upgrade history --last 1`
- `redwood-private diagnostics precheck --last` (or whatever the new command name is)
We should only document commands that exist in GA though.

Connor O'Brien: Agree. Also, we should explicitly validate versions:
- `redwood-private version`
- `kubectl -n redwood-system get deploy redwood-control-plane -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}'`
(or similar) so operators can confirm image tags.

Dev Patel: Can we avoid jsonpath complexity for customers? If installer can print “running version: X” that’s better. If not, keep jsonpath but with copy/pasteable examples.

Owen Phillips: For “smoke test” we need a safe, minimal call. Something like:
- hit the internal `/healthz` for API gateway
- run `redwood-private smoke-test --route chat --model <default>`
Do we have that command? If not, provide curl example.

Elliot Price: From docs POV: please define success criteria in plain language BEFORE the commands. Operators skim. Something like “Upgrade is successful when: (1) installer reports Completed, (2) all system pods are Ready, (3) migrations completed, (4) Redwood API returns 200 for /healthz and a small inference request, (5) no critical alerts.” Then list the exact checks.

Sean Gallagher: Also define rollback trigger points in the verification section:
- migrations failed / stuck beyond timeout
- control plane not ready after X mins
- error rate sustained above Y% (if they have metrics)
But keep it as “recommended rollback” vs “mandatory” depending on severity.

Rafael Mendes: Re: thresholds, we can phrase:
- If CP deployments not Available after 15 min post-apply -> stop and investigate; if still not progressing after 30 min -> recommend rollback.
- If migration job failed -> recommend rollback unless support advises otherwise.

Kira Thompson: Got it. I’ll update runbook with:
1) Installer verification: `redwood-private upgrade history --last 1` + show “Completed” + target version
2) K8s health: `kubectl get pods -n redwood-system` (Ready), `kubectl get jobs -n redwood-system` (migrations complete)
3) Helm deployed: `helm list -n redwood-system` + `helm history ...`
4) API health + smoke test: `/healthz` + one small inference request
5) Optional infra checks: node pressure, storage headroom
And a boxed “What counts as success / when to rollback” section.

Connor O'Brien: Please also include a note that warnings from prechecks must be explicitly acknowledged in the change record (esp storage headroom / OIDC reachability). That’s been biting us.

Sean Gallagher: +1. Also add “capture artifacts”: attach the upgrade plan + last 200 lines of installer logs to the ticket/support bundle.

Dev Patel: If you include that, Support will love you. :thumbsup:

Kira Thompson: Cool. I’ll push a revised draft today and tag @Connor O'Brien @Sean Gallagher for final review.

Rafael Mendes: Thanks. When you update, can you also add a one-liner that data plane “success” = inference workers ready AND serving (not just Ready). I’ve seen Ready pods that aren’t receiving traffic due to Service selector mismatch.

Ethan Park: +1. Maybe the smoke test hits both CP + DP paths implicitly.

Kira Thompson: Yep, will do.

Talia Benson: hey folks — quick cross-team review request 🙏

We have 2 ADRs that need final eyes today/tomorrow so we can lock semantics before ENG-4810/ENG-4812 go too deep.

1) admission control semantics ADR (what is a “burst request”, deny behavior, determinism guarantees)
2) fairness model ADR (shared burst pool fairness + anti-starvation)

Links:
- https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/adr-burst-pool-admission-control-semantics
- https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/adr-fairness-model-for-shared-burst-pools

Would love comments from control plane + routing + SRE + infra esp on:
- deny semantics: immediate vs queue/backpressure
- determinism under telemetry lag / retries
- fairness: token bucket vs WFQ hybrid, and “gold/silver” tiering guardrails

If you’re good w/ it, please reply “approved” here so I can note sign-off in the ADR.

Jordan Lee: reading now. quick q on ADR #1: we’re claiming “deterministic” decisions given same inputs — are we explicitly defining the input snapshot (telemetry + config) as-of request arrival? otherwise we’ll get people arguing about jitter.

Ethan Park: +1. we should scope determinism to policy eval (config + request attrs + latest capacity snapshot) and not promise global determinism across replicas unless we pin snapshot version.

Mina Haddad: skimming. on deny semantics: ADR says default is immediate deny w/ explicit reason code, optional short queue for “near-admit” situations. For routing, can we make that binary for v1? queueing complicates client streaming + gateway timeouts.

Ravi Menon: agree w/ Mina. I’d prefer:
- v1: immediate deny w/ `BURST_POOL_SATURATED` / `TENANT_CAP_REACHED` / `FLEET_SAFETY_OPEN`
- routing decides fallback (to baseline) or backpressure (429) based on route policy
If we later add queue, we need a dedicated timeout budget and consistent client retry guidance.

Keiko Yamamoto: on fairness ADR: token bucket per tenant w/ refill rate tied to burst limit makes sense. but we need to be explicit about cooldown windows — otherwise tenants can thrash burst on/off and still create neighbor pain.

Vanessa Ortiz: infra POV: please keep it simple for GA. shared burst pool sizing assumes “short duration” bursts + guardrails. queueing behavior risks longer hold times and could blow up pool occupancy in weird ways.

Sean Gallagher: SRE review:
- make sure ADR #1 references the SLO gate behavior (auto-reduce/disable burst when non-burst p95 regresses)
- denial semantics need to be *observable*: stable reason codes + counters (by tenant/tier, but w/ label cardinality constraints)
Other than that, seems aligned w/ what we discussed in eng-sre.

Allison Grant: observability nit: in ADR #1, “deterministic denial reason” should be stated as: reason code is derived from ordered evaluation of guardrails (tenant cap before fleet safety? or vice versa). otherwise you’ll see flapping reason distributions depending on which check fires first.

Jordan Lee: good call from Allison. We should define evaluation order in policy engine so reason code is stable.

Confluence Bot: Talia Benson shared a link: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/adr-burst-pool-admission-control-semantics

Lauren Bishop: following. +1 on keeping v1 simple/defensible for customer comms. immediate deny w/ clear reason is easiest to explain vs “you were queued but timed out”.

Talia Benson: 👍 on all of this. Proposed edits based on comments:
- clarify determinism scope: “deterministic given (request attrs, tenant config version, capacity snapshot version)”
- explicitly define policy eval order to stabilize reason codes
- v1 remove optional queue language; state “immediate admit/deny” and routing handles fallback/backpressure
- add pointer to SLO regression gate + global circuit breaker behavior

I’ll push updates shortly — please re-check after.

Ethan Park: works for me. one more small thing: mention idempotency key influence on retries (so we don’t double-count + also keep decisions consistent for same request id within TTL).

Ravi Menon: +1. routing will propagate `x-request-id` / idempotency; admission controller should treat it as advisory for decision caching.

Keiko Yamamoto: after those edits, fairness ADR is ✅ from me.

Vanessa Ortiz: same. once queue language is out and cooldown is crisp, infra approves.

Sean Gallagher: ok from SRE side w/ the added SLO gate reference + stable reasons.

Allison Grant: approved pending the “eval order” section. also please keep metric labels bounded (tier yes, tenant id no) — but that’s more in metrics doc than ADR.

Jordan Lee: I’ll add a short “decision cache TTL + snapshot versioning” section in ADR #1 so it’s unambiguous. then I’m approved.

Talia Benson: awesome. will update ADRs now and circle back here when done.

Talia Benson: updates are live in both ADRs (sections: Determinism & Inputs, Evaluation Order / Reason Codes, No-Queue v1 semantics, Retry/Idempotency notes, SLO gate references).
Can folks drop final “approved” so I can mark complete?

Keiko Yamamoto: approved

Vanessa Ortiz: approved

Sean Gallagher: approved

Allison Grant: approved

Ethan Park: approved

Mina Haddad: approved (thank you for removing queue from v1 🙏)

Ravi Menon: approved

Jordan Lee: approved

Connor O'Brien: heads up — we’re trying to land the Private upgrade/rollback installer PR stack this week. Can I get reviewers from Platform + SRE + Sec on these?

Connor O'Brien: Installer PRs:
- PR #4821 upgrade plan generator
- PR #4834 preflight check framework
- PR #4850 backup orchestrator + snapshot hooks
- PR #4862 rollback command + state machine
- PR #4871 airgapped bundle verification (checksums/signatures)

Connor O'Brien: TL;DR: need at least 1 approver from each: Release Eng (me), SRE, Platform Control Plane, Infra (storage), Security (auditability / backup handling). Also trying to keep the CLI UX consistent w/ DES-311.

GitHub Bot: Connor O'Brien shared links:
https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-private-installer/pull/4821
https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-private-installer/pull/4834
https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-private-installer/pull/4850
https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-private-installer/pull/4862
https://github.com/redwood-inference/redwood-private-installer/pull/4871

Ethan Park: I can take #4821 and #4862 (plan + rollback semantics). Anything specific you want me to look for besides state machine + persisted upgrade state?

Connor O'Brien: @Ethan Park yep — focus areas:
1) plan output is deterministic + stable (auditable)
2) rollback entry points line up w/ helm ordering (CP before DP)
3) “unsupported path” errors are actionable (ties to compat policy in redwood repo)

Sean Gallagher: I’ll review #4834 + #4862 from SRE angle. Primary concerns: prechecks severity (block vs warn), timeouts, and whether rollback leaves cluster in “healthy enough” state.

Rafael Mendes: can also do a pass on #4862. want to make sure it’s idempotent + safe to re-run (on-call reality).

Irene Choi: I’ll review #4850. Quick question: for snapshots, are we hard-requiring CSI snapshot API or do we support a logical backup fallback?

Connor O'Brien: @Irene Choi both. #4850 adds a storage backend abstraction:
- if CSI snapshot class present -> snapshot
- else -> logical backup path (db dump) w/ explicit warnings
on-prem folks asked for that fallback.

Sahana Iyer: I can sanity check #4834 from CI/testability perspective. If the prechecks return structured output (json), we can assert categories in e2e.

Vivek Kulkarni: following. for beta partners we need the reviewer coverage + a clear “what changed” for operators. also pls keep error messages copy/pasteable for support.

Kenji Nakamura: I’ll take a look at CLI output for #4821/#4834 (progress reporting + formatting). Do we have a standard for “remediation” blocks?

Connor O'Brien: @Kenji Nakamura we’re trying this pattern:
- one-line summary
- “Why this matters”
- “How to fix” w/ bullet steps
- optional link to runbook section

Allison Grant: I can do a quick pass for observability hooks. Do these PRs emit consistent step markers so dashboards can break down failures by phase?

Connor O'Brien: @Allison Grant good call — #4821 writes an upgrade plan artifact + step IDs, and #4862 writes rollback step IDs. We’re not emitting metrics directly here but we log structured events.

Naomi Feldman: I’ll review #4850 and #4871 for security/compliance.
Specifically:
- backup encryption expectations (KMS/HSM integrations) + no secrets in logs
- signature/checksum verification failure modes (don’t allow bypass w/o explicit flag)

Marcus Lin: I can back Naomi on #4871. Mostly want to check the crypto choices + key handling + what gets persisted on disk.

Dev Patel: support POV: can someone tag me in the PRs where the CLI errors change? we need to update internal troubleshooting macros.

Connor O'Brien: @Dev Patel will do. Biggest customer-facing changes are #4834 (precheck failures) + #4871 (bundle verification messages). I’ll drop screenshots in the PR descriptions.

Bruno Silva: I can look at #4871 too from on-prem constraints. Some customers don’t have gpg tooling; is verification built-in or shelling out?

Connor O'Brien: @Bruno Silva built-in. No external gpg dependency. Bundle includes manifest + signatures; installer verifies w/ embedded public keys + optional custom key via config.

Ethan Park: one more question: where is the “supported upgrade path” logic enforced? I see installer checks, but are we also gating in control plane?

Connor O'Brien: installer will block unsupported by default (with override flag for break-glass). control-plane policy is in separate PR (redwood repo) but we’re aligning error codes so support can correlate.

Sean Gallagher: request: can we make precheck results always print a final summary (PASS/WARN/BLOCK) even on early exit? helps when folks paste logs in tickets.

Connor O'Brien: yep. #4834 currently short-circuits on BLOCK but still prints the aggregated table at end (even if we exit non-zero). If it’s not, I’ll fix.

Irene Choi: for #4850 — do we record backup verification steps (restore dry-run?) anywhere? or just “snapshot created”? compliance customers will ask.

Connor O'Brien: good catch. current implementation does:
- snapshot/backup created
- then runs a minimal “backup integrity” check (db connect + expected tables count) when logical backup
for snapshots, we record snapshot IDs + storage class + timestamps. full restore validation is a runbook step for now.

Naomi Feldman: please ensure the audit trail captures: upgrade started, prechecks pass/fail, backup complete, rollback invoked. If this is only logs, we need a clear retention story.

Connor O'Brien: acknowledged. Audit events are in a separate platform PR, but installer will also persist the plan + results locally for support bundles.

Devon Price: I can skim for “support bundle” output location / naming. If it’s stable, we can auto-collect in internal tooling.

Connor O'Brien: thanks. Plan is under `/var/lib/redwood/upgrade/` (configurable) with `plan.json`, `prechecks.json`, `backup.json`, `execution.log`.

Rafael Mendes: :+1: on stable paths. Also make sure rollback writes `rollback.json` w/ status so on-call doesn’t have to infer from logs.

Connor O'Brien: noted — #4862 does write rollback execution summary. I’ll confirm filenames and document.

Ethan Park: ok, I’ll start review on #4821 now and follow with #4862. If I find anything weird w/ CP/DP ordering I’ll ping @Connor O'Brien and @Sean Gallagher.

Sean Gallagher: same. I’ll focus on failure modes + safe defaults. If we need last-minute tweaks, better today than during beta.

Connor O'Brien: appreciate it. Goal: initial approvals by EOD tomorrow so we can run the N-1→N upgrade e2e suite overnight. :pray:

Ethan Park: We need to lock down which prechecks are BLOCK vs WARN for Private upgrades. Specifically storage headroom, DB connectivity, OIDC reachability. Can we align today so the framework defaults aren’t bikesheddy?

Irene Choi: +1. Also important for on-prem. If we block too much, upgrades never run. If we warn too much, we get paging.

Michael Nguyen: For DB connectivity: IMO must-block. If installer can’t connect to the control-plane DB (or whatever migration target), upgrade will fail mid-flight anyway.

Sean Gallagher: Agree. DB connectivity is a hard dependency. If it flakes during upgrade window we’ll see partial state + rollback pain.

Rafael Mendes: Blocking for DB connectivity, and make the error message *actionable* (DNS, network policy, creds, TLS). Support will ask for that 100%.

Ethan Park: Sounds unanimous: DB connectivity = BLOCK.

Ethan Park: Storage headroom: question is “how strict” + what metric. Disk pressure tends to cause weird failures, but also customers always run hot.

Irene Choi: For storage, I’d block if we can’t safely take backups/snapshots. If backup target needs X GiB free and we have less than that, continuing is irresponsible.

Bruno Silva: +1. In VPC installs, we can usually query PVC capacity + usage and node disk. On-prem varies but still better to block when obviously impossible.

Fatima Noor: On-prem nuance: some environments have thin-provisioned storage and usage reporting is… optimistic. If we block purely on “reported usage,” we’ll false positive.

Sahana Iyer: From CI side, easiest if it’s deterministic: “requires >= N% free OR >= N GiB free”. But we need to define N.

Connor O'Brien: Release Eng POV: storage headroom should be BLOCK with a reasonable threshold and an override flag that is loud (requires `--force` + typed confirmation). We want customers to have an escape hatch but not by accident.

Sean Gallagher: Suggestion:
- BLOCK if < 10% free OR < 50Gi free (whichever is larger) on the volume(s) used by: DB storage, control-plane state, and any backup staging.
- WARN if between 10–20% free.
We can tune later, but at least it’s predictable.

Michael Nguyen: We also need to include migration temp usage. Some migrations create indexes -> temporary space spikes. So “current free” might not be enough.

Irene Choi: Yeah. Can we tie it to planned steps? e.g. “DB migration stage expects temp usage up to 1.3x DB size” is hard though.

Rafael Mendes: Don’t overfit for v1. We just need: “will backup succeed?” + “are we at high risk of disk pressure?”
If customer is below block threshold, they should expand volume or prune.

Ethan Park: Ok, I’m hearing: Storage headroom = BLOCK when it’s clearly insufficient for backup/upgrade safety, WARN when it’s tight but not catastrophic. We’ll define an initial threshold and allow explicit override.

Ethan Park: OIDC reachability is the tricky one. Do we block if we can’t reach the IdP/JWKS endpoint?

Victor Alvarez: Networking angle: in on-prem, outbound to IdP may be intentionally restricted except from certain subnets. “Installer pod can’t reach OIDC” might not mean the *running* system can’t (different egress rules).

Fatima Noor: Also some customers are using static admin tokens locally during maintenance, OIDC is “nice to have” but not required to complete upgrade. If we block, they’ll hate us.

Sean Gallagher: Counterpoint: if OIDC is required for day-2 login and it’s broken post-upgrade, ops can’t authenticate to fix anything. That becomes a sev-1 “locked out” scenario.

Michael Nguyen: Maybe conditional severity:
- If auth mode is OIDC-only (no break-glass local admin), then OIDC reachability = BLOCK.
- If we detect local admin/break-glass configured, OIDC reachability = WARN.

Irene Choi: +1. And we should be explicit in output: “OIDC unreachable from installer context. This may be expected in air-gapped envs; verify reachability from control-plane pods or ensure break-glass admin.”

Bruno Silva: We can implement check as:
- Attempt fetch of discovery doc + JWKS (`/.well-known/openid-configuration` then jwks_uri)
- Validate DNS + TLS chain
But allow `--skip-precheck oidc_reachability` only if `--ack-risk` or similar.

Connor O'Brien: I’m ok with WARN by default unless config says OIDC is the only auth path. The release gating doc will call out “do not proceed without working auth.”

Rafael Mendes: Strong request: if it’s a WARN, add a line that says *exactly* what will break: “SSO login to Console/API may fail; keep a local admin credential before proceeding.” That reduces support loops.

Sahana Iyer: For test matrix: we should cover both cases (OIDC required vs optional) so we don’t regress this logic.

Ethan Park: Quick strawman classification:
1) DB connectivity (target DB for CP state/migrations) => BLOCK
2) Storage headroom (backup + core PVCs) => BLOCK under threshold, WARN near threshold
3) OIDC reachability => WARN unless OIDC-only mode enabled (then BLOCK)
Any objections?

Vivek Kulkarni: Works for product. This aligns with “safe by default” without making on-prem impossible. Please make sure the CLI summary shows BLOCK vs WARN counts clearly.

Victor Alvarez: No objection. Just don’t make the OIDC check assume outbound internet. It should respect configured proxy/no_proxy too.

Irene Choi: +1 on proxy support. Also please include remediation hint: “check NetworkPolicy/egress, proxy env vars, DNS, cert bundle.”

Michael Nguyen: I’ll update ENG-4829 with this decision + draft messages. Also will add a follow-up for a “break-glass admin present?” detector.

Sean Gallagher: One more: for storage WARN, we should emit a metric/event so we can see how often customers are living dangerously.

Ethan Park: Cool. Action items:
- Michael: update ENG-4829 + precheck severities + messages
- Victor/Irene: review OIDC reachability implementation details (proxy/egress caveats)
- Connor: confirm the `--force` / override semantics are acceptable for release gating

Ethan Park: Thanks all.

source.text:
Rafael Mendes: Heads up: scheduling a Private DR game day rehearsal next week. Need to lock scenario + roles today/tomorrow so we can prep runbooks + staging env. Two candidates: (A) region loss / restore into clean cluster, or (B) cluster corruption (control plane state / k8s etcd issues). Thoughts? Also need IC/comms/scribe + operators.

Talia Benson: :eyes: What day/time are you aiming for? We should pick something that doesn’t overlap w/ release cut.

Rafael Mendes: Tentative Wed 10:00–12:00 PT. Will confirm once we choose scenario.

Sean Gallagher: For usefulness, I’d vote B first. We’ve had more “restore drill failed because cluster is weird” than actual region loss. But we can simulate region loss by treating it as “new cluster” anyway.

Miles Thornton: +1 that “region loss” is mostly an infra story (DNS/TLS, new cluster bootstrap). “Cluster corruption” forces us to prove the operator workflow: identify blast radius, decide etcd restore vs app-level restore, verify tenancy/routing policies.

Ethan Park: If we do B, can we constrain it to control-plane datastore corruption (e.g., config DB / state) rather than full etcd? Full etcd restore gets hairy and may turn into infra-only exercise.

Sean Gallagher: Agree. If the objective is validating our backup artifact + installer `restore apply`, we should avoid etcd snapshot restore unless we *explicitly* want to test infra runbook.

Neha Kapoor: What success metric are we measuring? If it’s RTO for “Private control plane back online + can serve requests”, then we should simulate loss of control-plane state and force a deterministic restore. Otherwise we’ll spend 2h debating k8s.

Rafael Mendes: Goal is: prove Day-2 toolkit + runbooks for enterprise. So: consistent backup, restore into fresh cluster, validate.

Vivek Kulkarni: From customer angle, “region loss” wording resonates. But if we can implement it as “we lost the cluster/control plane, here’s how we restore elsewhere” that’s fine. In VPC docs we’re positioning a same-region clean cluster restore as baseline.

Colin O'Donnell: If we pick “region loss” in the narrative, we should include DNS/TLS steps in the checklist. Even in staging, we can fake it w/ internal DNS record + cert rotation note.

Kira Thompson: I can draft the comms + run-of-show, but I need crisp scope: are we expecting people to touch IAM/KMS setup during the drill? Or assume backup bucket + KMS are already provisioned.

Hanae Suzuki: Please assume KMS is provisioned. Otherwise the drill becomes “debug IAM” for 90 min. We can still include a check that restore fails safely if decrypt can’t happen.

Marcus Lin: +1. Also we should explicitly state we are NOT restoring secret material, just secret refs, so we don’t end up passing around decrypted payloads in Slack.

Connor O'Brien: For on-prem/air-gapped, we still need to validate signed bundle verification at least once, but that might be a separate game day. For this one (VPC), we can keep it simple.

Fatima Noor: If we’re skipping air-gapped this time, can we at least add one “offline artifact import” step as optional? Like: download artifact to local runner, re-upload, then restore. We’ve seen customer workflows where bucket access is segmented.

Sean Gallagher: That’s a good “inject friction” step without turning it into full air-gap.

Allison Grant: Observability angle: can we make sure the drill outputs a single “validation report” artifact (json/text) + emits metrics (duration, success/fail, error class)? I want something we can pin to the dashboard later.

Sergio Costa: I can be on point for dashboards/metrics. If we know the scenario, I’ll pre-create a temp dashboard panel set for staging + alert rules disabled.

Rafael Mendes: Ok, proposed: Scenario narrative = “region loss” (primary cluster unavailable). Implementation = restore into clean cluster in same cloud account + region (to avoid cross-region IAM issues), using latest backup artifact. Injected failure = first restore attempt fails due to missing CRD/ordering (to test runbook steps).

Ethan Park: Missing CRD injection is realistic (we’ve seen it). But can we ensure it doesn’t require code changes mid-drill? We can just deploy the target cluster with an intentionally incomplete Helm install.

Sean Gallagher: We can do that. Staging target cluster: install base chart but omit `redwood-private-crds` (or whatever the package is called) and see if the restore tool catches it in preflight.

Marcus Lin: If preflight doesn’t catch it, that’s also useful info. But please don’t make it too “gotcha” unless we tell operators what failure we’re simulating.

Neha Kapoor: Agree. Failure injection should be discoverable via runbook, not tribal knowledge.

Talia Benson: Roles:
- IC: Rafael
- Ops lead (backup/restore commands): Sean?
- Control plane eng support: Ethan
- Security/KMS consult: Hanae
- Comms/scribe: Kira (or I can do scribe)
- Observability: Sergio
- Timekeeper: me
Does that work?

Rafael Mendes: Works for me. Sean, can you do ops lead?

Sean Gallagher: Yep. I’ll run the commands + narrate.

Ethan Park: I’ll be on standby to answer “what does this restore step do” and help interpret failures.

Hanae Suzuki: I’ll cover KMS/decrypt + key rotation questions if they come up.

Kira Thompson: I can do comms + scribe. Please confirm the audience: just eng/sre, or include Solutions?

Vivek Kulkarni: Would be great to include at least one Solutions person as observer (Markus/Colin). But keep it mostly internal so we can move fast.

Colin O'Donnell: I’ll join as observer and sanity-check steps against customer expectations.

Elliot Price: If there’s a doc gap surfaced, tag me and I’ll translate into a docs task. I’ll observe.

Allison Grant: Can we define explicit “done” criteria now?
1) Backup exists and passes integrity verify
2) Restore completes in <X minutes
3) Post-restore validation: API health, at least one test tenant config present, routing policy present, can generate a token (?)
4) Dashboard shows restore drill success + duration

Sean Gallagher: +1. Also add “abort criteria” so we don’t burn 3h: e.g., if restore blocked by infra provisioning, we pause and file ticket.

Rafael Mendes: Draft success criteria:
- RPO: latest backup <= 24h old (for drill)
- RTO target: control plane healthy + smoke tests green within 60 min from “declare disaster”
- Validation: installer validation report is PASS; API health endpoints green; sample tenant + routing policy restored; can run a simple inference request from staging runner.

Miles Thornton: Add: “document decision point: when to use etcd restore vs app-level restore”. Even if we don’t execute etcd restore, we should practice the call.

Sean Gallagher: Good call. We’ll add a 5-min decision checkpoint early: “is this k8s corruption or app state loss?” For our scenario we choose app-level restore.

Talia Benson: Logistics: which env are we using? We should freeze changes during the window.

Rafael Mendes: Staging: `private-drill-usw2` (new). We’ll do change freeze during 9:30–12:30 PT.

Connor O'Brien: Please make sure the installer version + docs version match. Last time we had “restore flag renamed” drift. I can provide the pinned artifact (installer + checksum) for the drill.

Kira Thompson: Perfect. If you send me the pinned version + links, I’ll put them into the run-of-show doc.

Sergio Costa: If you can paste the environment name + namespace(s), I’ll wire metric selectors. Also: do we want to emit a synthetic “restore_drill” label so we don’t pollute normal backup SLO charts?

Allison Grant: Yes, label it. `drill=true` or `event=dr_game_day`.

Rafael Mendes: Action items:
- Sean: propose exact step list + preflight checks by EOD
- Connor: pinned installer artifact + checksums by EOD
- Sergio/Allison: define metrics + minimal dashboard for the drill
- Kira: comms + run-of-show doc (roles, timeline, success/abort)
- Ethan/Hanae/Marcus: review failure injection + ensure we’re not handling secrets unsafely

Rafael Mendes: I’ll send calendar once we confirm scenario. :thumbsup:

(eng-bot): Reminder set by Talia Benson for “DR game day scenario locked + roles assigned” in 2 hours.

Marcus Lin: One more note: please avoid posting any real KMS key ARNs in thread; use redacted examples. Also make sure restore logs don’t dump decrypted headers.

Sean Gallagher: Ack. We’ll run everything from the staging runner and only paste sanitized snippets.

Talia Benson: Quick check: are we aligned on scenario name to share externally later? “Control plane restore to clean cluster (region loss simulation)”.

Vivek Kulkarni: Yep, that phrasing works.

Rafael Mendes: Locked. Scenario = “region loss simulation → restore into clean cluster”, with injected “missing CRD / ordering” failure. Roles as listed above.
