Logan Wright: not surprising. raw metering_events is partitioned by event_ts but we’re not getting good partition pruning b/c the query wraps timestamp filters in some date_trunc + timezone conversion.

Mei Lin: +1. In the query I saw `WHERE date_trunc('day', convert_timezone('UTC', :tz, event_ts)) BETWEEN ...` which forces compute on every row.

Dylan Brooks: yep. That’s from the API supporting timezone-based day boundaries. We can move TZ boundary logic to the app and pass UTC bounds?

Nadia Rahman: careful: finance wants day buckets aligned w/ workspace timezone for exports. but we can still pass UTC start/end and do the trunc in UTC if we precompute a “workspace_local_day” at ingest/agg time.

Thomas Baird: perf suggestion: don’t query raw for console. we should ship an aggregated fact table keyed by (workspace_id, project_id, hour_bucket/day_bucket, route_template, model_family, model_version_bucket, deployment_mode, region). Then the console query becomes a small scan + simple group/rollup.

Chloe Martin: we’re already implementing the aggregation job (ENG-3812). but current staging is still querying raw because the agg table is missing token breakdown + cache stats.

Mei Lin: we can extend the agg schema to include the expensive stuff so the breakdown query is a single table read:
- tokens_in, tokens_out
- cache_hit_tokens, cache_miss_tokens (or hit_rate numerator/denominator)
- retry_count, fallback_count
- streaming_request_count (or streaming_tokens_out)
- latency_ms_sum + latency_ms_p50/p95? (latency percentiles are tricky)

Allison Grant: for latency, we probably don’t want to compute percentiles ad hoc for every filter. if we need p95 in-console, either:
1) maintain t-digest sketches per key per hour/day (warehouse-specific), or
2) precompute fixed buckets (e.g., <250ms, 250-500, 500-1s, 1-2s, 2-5s, >5s) and derive “latency-correlated spend” from those.

Felix Schneider: right now the query is doing a join against `request_latency_events` anyway. it’s another full scan.

Logan Wright: materialized view option: create MV `usage_insights_route_model_hourly` from metering_events w/ the dims we care about + route normalized at write time. then query service just filters + aggregates to requested time grain.

Thomas Baird: +1, MV or scheduled job. if we go w/ job, do hourly partitions + cluster by workspace_id, project_id, route_template. that should keep scans bounded.

Dylan Brooks: do we have numbers on cardinality? route_template might blow up if normalization misses.

Chloe Martin: in staging data, top offenders are OpenAI-compat endpoints where path params aren’t templated consistently. That’s in ENG-3813. once we canonicalize, route cardinality is manageable.

Nadia Rahman: also model aliases are causing duplicates right now. if we aggregate on raw model_id, we’ll still have weirdness. we should aggregate on (model_family, model_version_bucket) and keep model_id for drilldown only.

Mei Lin: +1. plus, we can keep a separate “model_id -> bucket” mapping table and update it, but aggregation should store the bucketed dims for stable reporting.

Felix Schneider: tactical fix for this week: change the breakdown query to hit the agg table for tokens + cost, and only join raw for the “what changed” explanations if needed.

Allison Grant: i’d rather we don’t have the console path ever touching raw for beta. observability-wise it’s hard to SLO if query shape changes per filter.

Logan Wright: another easy win: restrict time ranges for interactive queries. e.g. hard cap 14d for breakdown view unless user requests export (async). export can run heavier queries w/ longer timeout.

Dylan Brooks: product will push back on caps but we can justify with “interactive perf.” export endpoint can do 30d+.

Chloe Martin: also pagination. we’re currently doing `ORDER BY cost DESC LIMIT 500` but without pre-aggregation it still computes global group-by first. With pre-agg it becomes cheap.

Thomas Baird: indexes / clustering keys matter: if warehouse supports it, cluster on (workspace_id, day_bucket) then secondary on (route_template, model_bucket). most queries are constrained by workspace + time.

Mei Lin: and don’t compute “totals” as a separate query. we can add a rollup row in the agg table or compute it from same dataset (less round trips).

Felix Schneider: i’ll paste the worst offender query plan summary:
```
Scan metering_events (partitioned by event_ts): 29,184 partitions
Filter: computed expression on event_ts prevents pruning
Join route_normalized_view: broadcast
Group by: workspace_id, project_id, route, model
Distinct: request_id
Spill: yes (disk)
Total time: 48.2s (timed out at 60s)
```

Dylan Brooks: ok. action items?

Logan Wright: propose:
1) adjust API to pass UTC bounds, no tz conversion inside WHERE
2) prioritize MV/job for `usage_insights_route_model_hourly` including tokens/cache/retries/fallback/streaming counts
3) add clustering/partition scheme + backfill last 30d for beta accounts only
4) for latency: choose histogram buckets (fast) now; consider t-digest later

Allison Grant: add alerting around agg lag + query p95. if we’re doing MVs, we need freshness indicators in console anyway.

Chloe Martin: i’ll take (1) + coordinate with Felix on query service changes. Logan/Mei can you spec the MV/job columns today so we can update ENG-3812?

Mei Lin: yep, I’ll post a proposed schema in the ENG-3812 ticket comments.

Thomas Baird: i can run a quick benchmark once schema is set. we should test 7d/30d scans on a “big” workspace and confirm p95 < 1.5s for interactive.

Dylan Brooks: 👍 let’s do it. also: for beta, we can message “30d+ recommended via export.” will sync w/ Marissa/Paula.

Nadia Rahman: one more note: reconciliation vs billing—ensure aggregation uses the same rounding policy as billing hooks (or stores unrounded units and only rounds at display/export). don’t let perf changes break correctness.

Chloe Martin: good call. we’ll store raw microdollars/token_units in agg and format at the edge.

Felix Schneider: I’ll open a PR to remove timezone transforms from WHERE and to enforce a max time range for interactive endpoints (configurable) until MV is live.

Allison Grant: please also log `warehouse_bytes_scanned` and `warehouse_query_time_ms` per request in the query service. makes perf regressions obvious.

Dylan Brooks: done. closing loop once we have new benchmark numbers.

source.thread:
Chloe Martin: Heads up, I’m still worried about correctness for Usage Insights before we show this to customers. Two things keep coming up in review: (1) totals vs invoice (rounding / timing) and (2) what we call a “retry” in attribution. Can we align on semantics?

Cole Summers: +1. Billing side: invoice totals are computed from metering events with unrounded USD at event-level, then we round at the invoice line item / total. If Insights aggregates per-route then rounds each row, we’ll drift.

Felix Schneider: Right now UI table rounds `cost_usd` to 2 decimals for display and export (CSV) uses 2 decimals too (based on the working spec). That’s almost guaranteed to mismatch when you sum.

Nadia Rahman: On pipeline side we can store `cost_usd_unrounded` at 1e-6 and only round at the very end (display). For export, I’d prefer we include both `cost_usd` (2dp) and `cost_usd_unrounded` (6dp) so finance can reconcile.

Dylan Brooks: Export spec currently says 2 decimals because finance folks want “money-looking” columns. But if it creates SUP tickets, we should change. Maybe add “Display Cost (rounded)” and “Cost (unrounded)” columns.

Cole Summers: Please. Billing reconciliation should be based on unrounded. If we only export rounded cents, it’s not reconcilable.

Chloe Martin: Ok, rounding: keep aggregation in micros, display rounds. And we’ll communicate “Totals may differ due to rounding” only if still needed.

Allison Grant: Also timing: invoice totals are based on billed time windows in UTC + cutoffs (late events handled differently). Insights is near-real-time with late arrivals. We should be explicit about freshness + “partial data” banners.

Logan Wright: For the dataset, we have late-event handling (up to 24h) and we recompute affected buckets. But the console can query “last 30 days” and will include buckets still settling. That’s where drift comes from.

Thomas Baird: Can we define a reconciliation policy for “settled windows”? e.g., only assert parity for days older than 48h.

Cole Summers: That’s reasonable. Billing has a 36h-ish lag for some regions, so 48h gives buffer.

Chloe Martin: Let’s capture this as: “Reconciliation SLO: for windows ending >48h ago, Insights total matches Billing total within tolerance.” What tolerance?

Cole Summers: Ideally 0.0 with same source-of-truth, but because of model alias bucketing + route normalization changes we might have temporary drift. I’d say 0.2% or $1 (whichever larger) during beta, tighten later.

Allison Grant: For beta, 0.2% seems ok but we need alerts when it breaches, and the UI should never silently show wrong numbers without a warning.

Felix Schneider: We can surface a banner when reconciliation job flags drift for selected time range (or at least for “Last 7 days”). Might be too heavy for phase 1 though.

Dylan Brooks: Maybe phase 1: always show “Data freshness: last updated X mins ago” and “Totals may change for recent hours.” Phase 2: show drift warnings based on reconciliation.

Chloe Martin: Cool. Now: “retry” definition.

Nadia Rahman: Current telemetry has `request_id` per gateway request. If the client retries, it’s a new `request_id`. If we internally retry upstream (e.g., transient backend error), it may keep the same `request_id` but we’ll have multiple attempts logged.

Logan Wright: In the aggregation PR we introduced `attempt_id` (uuid) per execution attempt. But not all emitters are populating it consistently yet.

Cole Summers: Billing charges for tokens actually generated/returned (and embeddings units), not for failed attempts with 0 tokens. But partial streaming responses complicate this: if we emitted some tokens, then the client disconnects, those tokens are billable today.

Dylan Brooks: UX question: when users see “retries increased,” they’ll assume “wasted spend.” But if we’re only counting attempts that generated tokens, it’s not wasted, it’s real output.

Allison Grant: We should separate “retry_count” (attempts) from “retry_cost_usd” (cost attributable to retry attempts). And define retry attempts as attempts beyond the first for the same client operation.

Chloe Martin: Do we have a stable “client operation id”? Something like `idempotency_key`?

Nadia Rahman: Sometimes. For SDKs we have `client_request_id` / idempotency key, but raw REST callers don’t always set it.

Thomas Baird: If no idempotency key, we can’t reliably tie multiple gateway requests together as a retry chain.

Cole Summers: Billing doesn’t need that. For Insights, I’d rather avoid a misleading “retry” metric if it’s half-guesses.

Felix Schneider: Current draft counts retries as `attempt_number > 1` within a single gateway request (same `request_id`). That captures internal retries (ex: rerun after a 5xx from a model server) but NOT client retries.

Dylan Brooks: That might actually be the safest definition for v1. We can label it “Internal retries” (or “Execution retries”) and tooltip it.

Allison Grant: +1, avoid conflating with client behavior. Also add “Fallbacks” separately.

Chloe Martin: Ok so for v1:
- Retry = additional execution attempts for same gateway request (`request_id`) (needs consistent `attempt_id` + `attempt_ordinal`).
- Client retries not counted unless we have idempotency key (future enhancement).

Nadia Rahman: Works. For cost attribution: If attempt 1 fails and attempt 2 succeeds, all tokens/cost are on attempt 2 but we still want to count “1 retry” as a driver.

Cole Summers: Careful: if attempt 1 generated partial tokens (streaming) and then failed, those tokens are billable too. So cost may span attempts.

Logan Wright: We have token counts per attempt in the runtime metrics. We can attribute `tokens_out` / `cost_usd_unrounded` per attempt, then aggregate. That lets us compute “retry_tokens_out” = sum(tokens_out for attempts > 1).

Felix Schneider: That’ll be great for top drivers: “Retries increased +X% and contributed $Y in extra output cost.”

Chloe Martin: What about fallbacks? If model A fails and we fallback to model B, is that a retry or a fallback?

Nadia Rahman: In our event schema it’s `fallback_reason` + `served_model`. We can treat fallback as separate dimension. For cost by model, we should attribute cost to the model that actually served tokens (B), but also count a fallback event so users understand mix changes.

Cole Summers: Agreed. Invoice is charged for served model. If we attribute cost to the requested model, we’ll mismatch totals per model.

Dylan Brooks: So tables: group by `served_model` (with alias bucketing) and show `requested_model` only in drilldown? Might be too much.

Chloe Martin: For beta, keep it simple: cost is by served model. We’ll include a “Fallbacks” driver to explain why served model mix changed.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: RBAC note: If we expose “retries” and “fallback reasons,” check if those fields are considered operational telemetry. Should be fine but we need to ensure exports don’t leak internal failure codes to non-admin roles.

Allison Grant: Good call. Use normalized categories for reasons (capacity, timeout, upstream_error) not raw stack strings.

Chloe Martin: Action items:
- Nadia + Logan: confirm attempt_id + attempt_ordinal availability end-to-end; define v1 retry metric as internal retries only.
- Cole: send billing rounding guidance (unrounded aggregation, rounding at display) + propose reconciliation tolerance and “settled window” rule.
- Dylan + Felix: update export spec to include unrounded cost column and adjust UI copy/tooltips (“Internal retries”).
- Allison: sanity check metric definitions for misleading interpretations + propose alert thresholds for drift.

Cole Summers: I’ll also add a note: exports should include currency and pricing version (if we ever change price tables) to prevent “same tokens different $” confusion.

Dylan Brooks: yep, add `currency=USD` and `pricing_effective_at` (or just `pricing_plan_id`).

Chloe Martin: Perfect. I’ll drop this in the ADR / API contract review thread as well.

source.messages:
Amina Yusuf: Working on private-installer audit events (upgrade/rollback/config apply). Main open question: how do we represent operator identity in Private when someone runs `redwood-private install/upgrade` from a bastion / laptop? We can’t always rely on OIDC.

Diego Santos: yeah this is the parity gap vs Hosted/Dedicated. Hosted we have `actor.user_id` + auth context + source_ip. In Private, “operator” might be (a) human running CLI, (b) automation (Ansible, Argo, etc). We should model both.

Vivek Kulkarni: +1. For enterprise customers, they keep asking “who did the upgrade” and “who changed exporter settings”. Even if it’s “unknown automation” we need something consistent.

Kevin Osei: Reminder from schema discussions: we need stable semantics, minimal PII, and good dedupe. For Private installer events, propose:
- `actor.type`: `human` | `service` | `unknown`
- `actor.id`: if known (OIDC sub, customer SSO GUID, or service principal)
- `actor.display_name`: optional, but would be high-cardinality + often PII. We should avoid unless we’re hashing.
- `actor.email_sha256`: optional if we have email, but only if customer opts in? (also doesn’t help if no email)

Hanae Suzuki: +1 avoid raw email. We already have redaction rules draft that treats email as PII. Hash is OK but still sensitive-ish; needs consistent salt story (can’t be Redwood secret in air-gapped env).

Amina Yusuf: Here’s what the installer currently can reliably collect:
- CLI has `--operator-id` (we can add) + `--operator-type` (human/service)
- In interactive mode, we can optionally prompt `operator` (freeform) but that’s the worst for cardinality
- In k8s, we can read `KUBECONFIG` context user name (but that’s messy / sometimes includes email)
- We always have `installer_instance_id` and `installer_version`

Rafael Mendes: From incident forensics POV, even “unknown” is better than nothing as long as we have correlation IDs + the host it ran on. Can we capture `execution_environment` like `host.hostname` or `node`? (but careful: could leak internal naming)

Sean Gallagher: SRE take: do NOT block upgrade/rollback on identity collection. During an outage we need to run rollback fast. Identity should be best-effort.

Diego Santos: Agree. We can treat actor fields as best-effort but the event itself must be emitted. We should add `actor.attribution`: `explicit` (flag provided) vs `inferred` (kube context) vs `unknown`.

Kevin Osei: That helps for customer guidance too: “If you want strong operator attribution, pass operator-id via automation or configure OIDC.”

Connor O'Brien: Release perspective: we should avoid adding an interactive prompt by default. Flags/env vars are safer for automation.

Amina Yusuf: Proposal v1 (please poke holes):
- Add flags/env:
  - `--actor-type` / `RW_AUDIT_ACTOR_TYPE` in {human, service}
  - `--actor-id` / `RW_AUDIT_ACTOR_ID` (string, recommended stable GUID)
  - `--actor-idp` / `RW_AUDIT_ACTOR_IDP` (optional, e.g. okta, aad, local)
- Installer emits audit events with:
  - `actor.type` from flag else `unknown`
  - `actor.id` from flag else empty
  - `actor.attribution` = explicit|unknown
  - `client.name` = `redwood-private-installer`, `client.version` = installer version
  - `source.ip` omitted (or null) in Private
- Add `operator` info as a namespaced extension field if we need more later (e.g. `private.operator.*`)

Hanae Suzuki: looks good. One tweak: if we include `actor.idp`, limit allowed values (enum) to avoid random strings -> cardinality.

Diego Santos: +1. Also for parity: use the same top-level `actor.*` fields as schema v1, don’t invent `operator.*` at top-level. Put anything installer-specific under `private_installer.*` extension.

Leila Farouk: For E2E tests, we can set `RW_AUDIT_ACTOR_TYPE=service` and `RW_AUDIT_ACTOR_ID=ci-fixture` and assert it shows up in the event payload. That’ll keep coverage tests deterministic.

Rafael Mendes: For rollback, make sure we emit BOTH attempt + result events (start/success/failure) so we can see failed rollbacks too. Operator identity should be copied across them.

Kevin Osei: and add `event_id` (ULID) + `admin_action_id` for the whole run so SIEM can group. Even if retries happen, de-dupe by `event_id`.

Amina Yusuf: Cool. I’ll implement:
- `admin_action_id` generated once per CLI invocation
- event types:
  - `private.installer.upgrade.started|completed|failed`
  - `private.installer.rollback.started|completed|failed`
  - `private.installer.config_apply.started|completed|failed`
- actor fields best-effort via flags/env; no kube-context sniffing in v1

DeployBot: DeployBot APP  [automated] queued checks for redwood-private-installer/pr-611-emit-audit-events-during-install-upgrade-rollback

Sean Gallagher: please add a short note in the runbook/docs: “If actor is unknown, look for bastion host logs / change ticket refs using admin_action_id”. Customers will ask.

Vivek Kulkarni: +1. And we should align wording with Security FAQ: “Private installer supports operator attribution via provided identifiers; otherwise events will show actor as unknown.” No promises beyond that.

Amina Yusuf: ack. I’ll drop a comment on PR with the proposed fields + ask Kevin/Hanae for schema sanity review.

Leila Farouk: For E2E tests, we can set `RW_AUDIT_ACTOR_TYPE=service` and `RW_AUDIT_ACTOR_ID=ci-fixture` and assert it shows up in the event payload. That’ll keep coverage tests deterministic.

Rafael Mendes: For rollback, make sure we emit BOTH attempt + result events (start/success/failure) so we can see failed rollbacks too. Operator identity should be copied across them.

Kevin Osei: and add `event_id` (ULID) + `admin_action_id` for the whole run so SIEM can group. Even if retries happen, de-dupe by `event_id`.

Amina Yusuf: Cool. I’ll implement:
- `admin_action_id` generated once per CLI invocation
- event types:
  - `private.installer.upgrade.started|completed|failed`
  - `private.installer.rollback.started|completed|failed`
  - `private.installer.config_apply.started|completed|failed`
- actor fields best-effort via flags/env; no kube-context sniffing in v1

DeployBot: DeployBot APP  [automated] queued checks for redwood-private-installer/pr-611-emit-audit-events-during-install-upgrade-rollback

Sean Gallagher: please add a short note in the runbook/docs: “If actor is unknown, look for bastion host logs / change ticket refs using admin_action_id”. Customers will ask.

Vivek Kulkarni: +1. And we should align wording with Security FAQ: “Private installer supports operator attribution via provided identifiers; otherwise events will show actor as unknown.” No promises beyond that.

Amina Yusuf: ack. I’ll drop a comment on PR with the proposed fields + ask Kevin/Hanae for schema sanity review.

source.messages:
Chloe Martin: dropping the latest API contract sketch for unit econ dashboard queries. would love review esp on pagination + caching approach. doc here: go/redwood/unit-econ-api-contract (WIP)

Chloe Martin: TL;DR proposal: single POST endpoint for both timeseries + breakdowns:
`POST /internal/unit-economics/query`

request shape (draft):
```
{
  "metric": "blended_cost_per_1m_output_tokens",
  "time": {"start": "2025-01-01T00:00:00Z", "end": "2025-01-21T00:00:00Z", "grain": "hour"},
  "filters": {"model": ["llama3.1-70b"], "region": ["us-east-1"], "tier": ["self_serve"], "quant_profile": ["fp16"]},
  "group_by": ["model", "region"],
  "order_by": [{"field": "metric", "dir": "desc"}],
  "limit": 50,
  "cursor": null
}
```
response:
```
{
  "data": [...],
  "next_cursor": "...",
  "meta": {"partial": false, "cache": "hit"}
}
```

Chloe Martin: caching: API layer caches common queries (TTL ~60s) and we also rely on warehouse materialized views for speed. pagination: cursor-based for breakdown tables. timeseries returns full series for requested range (bounded by max window).

Felix Schneider: thx. quick q: are we sure we want one endpoint for both timeseries + topN breakdown? seems flexible but UI will end up with 2-3 fixed query shapes anyway. single endpoint makes caching keys tricky.

Jada Williams: +1 on caching keys. also single endpoint tends to become “anything goes” and then we have to support weird combos (group_by time + other dims + order_by metric??)

Nora Ahmed: from UI side we’ll call:
1) timeseries (single line, optionally compare prev period)
2) topN models
3) topN regions
4) topN tiers
and then drill to “detail table” w pagination

Nora Ahmed: if we can have predictable endpoints, it helps with client caching + type safety.

Chloe Martin: fair. i was trying to avoid endpoint explosion but agree we should constrain query shapes. what would you prefer?

Mei Lin: data side: timeseries and breakdown hit different views. timeseries is basically `mv_unit_econ_hourly` aggregated by dims + hour. breakdown is same but order/limit. i’d rather we expose explicit query types so we can map to the right MV and keep p95 down.

Logan Wright: also want guardrails on window sizes. finance likes QTD which is fine, but if someone asks 365d hourly * group_by(model,region,quant_profile,tier) it’s going to wreck the warehouse.

Allison Grant: +1. please make max ranges explicit and return a helpful error. we’ll get paged for “dashboard slow” otherwise.

Chloe Martin: ok. proposed split:
- `GET /internal/unit-economics/timeseries`
- `GET /internal/unit-economics/breakdown`
where breakdown supports group_by + topN + pagination

Chloe Martin: question: keep POST or move to GET w query params? GET makes caching/CDN easier but filters are arrays.

Felix Schneider: i’d do POST for breakdown (complex body) and GET for timeseries (simple). timeseries params are manageable: metric, start/end, grain, plus filters (repeatable query params)

Jada Williams: if we do GET + arrays, we’ll end up with `model=...&model=...` anyway. i’m fine w that for timeseries.

Nora Ahmed: +1. timeseries is the one we’ll refresh frequently and want to cache aggressively.

Sean Gallagher: re caching: please don’t “cache everything 60s” by default. we need to be explicit about freshness expectations. finance view vs live view came up in SRE thread.

Allison Grant: yes. recommend two modes:
- `view=live` (low TTL, allows partial latest hour)
- `view=finance` (hourly finalized, longer TTL, no partial)
then our alerting can treat finance view as “source of truth”.

Chloe Martin: i saw that thread. we can add `view` param default `live` for eng users, and Finance can pin `finance`.

Logan Wright: “finance” should probably align to the hourly job watermark and only expose hours <= watermark. otherwise we’ll keep arguing about missing last 20 mins.

Mei Lin: +1. we can materialize `unit_econ_hourly_finance` which lags by 1-2 hours but is stable.

Chloe Martin: ok, so:
`GET /internal/unit-economics/timeseries?metric=...&start=...&end=...&grain=hour&view=finance&model=...&region=...&tier=...&quant_profile=...`

Chloe Martin: breakdown:
`POST /internal/unit-economics/breakdown`
body:
```
{
  "metric": "blended_cost_per_1m_output_tokens",
  "time": {"start": "...", "end": "...", "grain": "day"},
  "view": "finance",
  "filters": {"tier": ["self_serve", "dedicated"], "region": ["us-east-1"]},
  "group_by": ["model"],
  "sort": {"field": "metric", "dir": "desc"},
  "limit": 25,
  "cursor": null
}
```

Felix Schneider: pagination: please don’t do offset/limit. cursor should be stable based on sort key. but if sort key can tie (same metric), you need tie-breaker.

Jada Williams: +1. cursor should encode (sort_value, dimension_key) at least.

Chloe Martin: yep. proposal: order is (metric_value DESC, group_key ASC). cursor encodes last row’s metric_value + group_key. if group_by has multiple dims, group_key is a canonical string like `model=...|region=...|tier=...`.

Nora Ahmed: question: UI wants “Top 10 + Others” bar sometimes. do we get total count?

Chloe Martin: count is expensive. could return `meta.total_groups_estimate`?? or we can request `include_totals=true` for a slower path.

Mei Lin: pls don’t add totals v1 unless needed. counting distinct groups across huge windows is not cheap.

Felix Schneider: agree. for “Others”, UI can sum the returned topN and label remainder as “not shown”.

Nora Ahmed: works.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: authz note: these endpoints must be behind internal feature flag + RBAC. contract should include 403 vs 404 behavior (we typically return 404 for disabled feature, 403 for not authorized).

Chloe Martin: good call. i’ll follow the console pattern: 
- feature flag off => 404
- flag on but user not in allowlist => 403

Dylan Brooks: +1 and we should log access for auditing (at least user id + query type + dims requested). not row-level sensitive but still econ data.

Allison Grant: also rate limit. once finance starts pinning dashboards on TVs we’ll see bursts. suggest server-side caching per query key + a per-user/IP rate limit.

Sean Gallagher: +1. and please add circuit breaker behavior if warehouse is slow (serve stale cache for finance view is OK). for live view we can fail open with “data temporarily unavailable”.

Chloe Martin: caching policy suggestion:
- timeseries live: TTL 60s, stale-while-revalidate 30s
- timeseries finance: TTL 10m, stale-if-error 60m
- breakdown live: TTL 2m
- breakdown finance: TTL 15m
and hard max time range per grain (e.g. hour <= 31d, day <= 365d)

Felix Schneider: sounds reasonable. would also be nice if response includes `meta.watermark` (latest finalized hour) so UI can show “data through 10:00Z”.

Chloe Martin: yes adding `meta.watermark_ts`.

Logan Wright: please also surface `meta.partial=true` only for live view when latest hour incomplete, and never for finance.

Chloe Martin: ack.

Jada Williams: contract question: dimension names. we should align with token metering dimension standard (region_normalized, customer_tier, model_id, model_version, quant_profile). UI labels can map separately.

Mei Lin: strongly +1. also region should be normalized already. no raw cloud region strings.

Chloe Martin: ok: API exposes canonical dimension keys (e.g. `region`, `tier`, `model`, `model_version`, `quant_profile`). internally we map to warehouse columns `region_norm`, etc. (to avoid leaking internal column names). but values must be canonical, not aliases.

Nora Ahmed: will we allow grouping by model_version in v1? product asked for it but it increases cardinality.

Logan Wright: allow it but only when also filtering to a specific model family (or limit time range). otherwise it’s 500 rows per model.

Allison Grant: could enforce server-side rules: if group_by contains model_version then require filter.model present OR limit<=50 + short range.

Chloe Martin: yep, we can validate and return 400 with message.

Felix Schneider: for cursor stability, note float ordering issues. metric is a decimal. better to order by a rounded value or order by (numerator_cost, denominator_tokens)??

Mei Lin: warehouse stores cost as DECIMAL and tokens as BIGINT. metric computed as `cost / tokens * 1e6`. ordering by computed metric is fine if we order by (cost, tokens, group_key) maybe?

Jada Williams: easiest: in the view, compute `metric_value` as DECIMAL(38, 12) and order by that. cursor stores stringified decimal.

Chloe Martin: ok.

Sean Gallagher: operational note: include query fingerprint in logs + metrics (but careful about cardinality). ex: hash(metric + view + group_by + grain + filter_keys only). don’t put raw model IDs in labels.

Allison Grant: +1, we’ve been burned by metric label cardinality.

Chloe Martin: will do.

Dylan Brooks: seems directionally good. action items:
1) Chloe update doc w split endpoints + view param + watermark + validation rules
2) Felix/Jada sanity-check cursor encoding + tie-breaker
3) Allison/Sean review caching TTL + rate limits for SRE signoff

Chloe Martin: 👍 will post updated contract tomorrow.

Felix Schneider: thx. also pls add examples for:
- compare period (prev range)
- breakdown by (region,tier)
- error response for invalid range

Chloe Martin: noted.

[Next day]

Chloe Martin: updated doc w endpoints + examples: go/redwood/unit-econ-api-contract#v2
highlights:
- GET /timeseries (supports `compare=previous_period`)
- POST /breakdown (cursor pagination)
- `view=live|finance`, response includes `meta.watermark_ts`, `meta.partial`
- validation: max window + restricted group_by combos

Allison Grant: reviewed. caching policy looks ok. one tweak: for finance view, TTL 15m is fine but set `stale-if-error=24h` so we don’t flap exec review if warehouse hiccups.

Sean Gallagher: +1. also add explicit 503 vs 504 semantics? if warehouse timeout, return 503 with `meta.stale_served=true` when we serve stale cache.

Chloe Martin: cool. added `meta.stale_served` + `meta.cache_age_seconds`.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: RBAC section looks good. please ensure endpoints are under `/internal/...` and also gated by `X-Redwood-Internal: true` header like other internal console APIs.

Chloe Martin: yep.

Jada Williams: cursor encoding: please base64(json) not base64(“string|string”). easier to evolve. but we can still treat it as opaque.

Chloe Martin: changed to base64(JSON) with fields: `last_metric_value`, `last_group_key`. server treats unknown fields as ignore.

Mei Lin: 👍 from data side. when you implement, please default breakdown grain to `day` (hourly breakdown tables get big fast).

Nora Ahmed: works. UI will use day unless user toggles hour.

Dylan Brooks: looks ready for ENG-8427. ship it.

```
  "sort": {"field": "metric", "dir": "desc"},
  "limit": 25,
  "cursor": null
}
```

Felix Schneider: pagination: please don’t do offset/limit. cursor should be stable based on sort key. but if sort key can tie (same metric), you need tie-breaker.

Jada Williams: +1. cursor should encode (sort_value, dimension_key) at least.

Chloe Martin: yep. proposal: order is (metric_value DESC, group_key ASC). cursor encodes last row’s metric_value + group_key. if group_by has multiple dims, group_key is a canonical string like `model=...|region=...|tier=...`.

Nora Ahmed: question: UI wants “Top 10 + Others” bar sometimes. do we get total count?

Chloe Martin: count is expensive. could return `meta.total_groups_estimate`?? or we can request `include_totals=true` for a slower path.

Mei Lin: pls don’t add totals v1 unless needed. counting distinct groups across huge windows is not cheap.

Felix Schneider: agree. for “Others”, UI can sum the returned topN and label remainder as “not shown”.

Nora Ahmed: works.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: authz note: these endpoints must be behind internal feature flag + RBAC. contract should include 403 vs 404 behavior (we typically return 404 for disabled feature, 403 for not authorized).

Chloe Martin: good call. i’ll follow the console pattern: 
- feature flag off => 404
- flag on but user not in allowlist => 403

Dylan Brooks: +1 and we should log access for auditing (at least user id + query type + dims requested). not row-level sensitive but still econ data.

Allison Grant: also rate limit. once finance starts pinning dashboards on TVs we’ll see bursts. suggest server-side caching per query key + a per-user/IP rate limit.

Sean Gallagher: +1. and please add circuit breaker behavior if warehouse is slow (serve stale cache for finance view is OK). for live view we can fail open with “data temporarily unavailable”.

Chloe Martin: caching policy suggestion:
- timeseries live: TTL 60s, stale-while-revalidate 30s
- timeseries finance: TTL 10m, stale-if-error 60m
- breakdown live: TTL 2m
- breakdown finance: TTL 15m
and hard max time range per grain (e.g. hour <= 31d, day <= 365d)

Felix Schneider: sounds reasonable. would also be nice if response includes `meta.watermark` (latest finalized hour) so UI can show “data through 10:00Z”.

Chloe Martin: yes adding `meta.watermark_ts`.

Logan Wright: please also surface `meta.partial=true` only for live view when latest hour incomplete, and never for finance.

Chloe Martin: ack.

Jada Williams: contract question: dimension names. we should align with token metering dimension standard (region_normalized, customer_tier, model_id, model_version, quant_profile). UI labels can map separately.

Mei Lin: strongly +1. also region should be normalized already. no raw cloud region strings.

Chloe Martin: ok: API exposes canonical dimension keys (e.g. `region`, `tier`, `model`, `model_version`, `quant_profile`). internally we map to warehouse columns `region_norm`, etc. (to avoid leaking internal column names). but values must be canonical, not aliases.

Nora Ahmed: will we allow grouping by model_version in v1? product asked for it but it increases cardinality.

Logan Wright: allow it but only when also filtering to a specific model family (or limit time range). otherwise it’s 500 rows per model.

Allison Grant: could enforce server-side rules: if group_by contains model_version then require filter.model present OR limit<=50 + short range.

Chloe Martin: yep, we can validate and return 400 with message.

Felix Schneider: for cursor stability, note float ordering issues. metric is a decimal. better to order by a rounded value or order by (numerator_cost, denominator_tokens)??

Mei Lin: warehouse stores cost as DECIMAL and tokens as BIGINT. metric computed as `cost / tokens * 1e6`. ordering by computed metric is fine if we order by (cost, tokens, group_key) maybe?

Jada Williams: easiest: in the view, compute `metric_value` as DECIMAL(38, 12) and order by that. cursor stores stringified decimal.

Chloe Martin: ok.

Sean Gallagher: operational note: include query fingerprint in logs + metrics (but careful about cardinality). ex: hash(metric + view + group_by + grain + filter_keys only). don’t put raw model IDs in labels.

Allison Grant: +1, we’ve been burned by metric label cardinality.

Chloe Martin: will do.

Dylan Brooks: seems directionally good. action items:
1) Chloe update doc w split endpoints + view param + watermark + validation rules
2) Felix/Jada sanity-check cursor encoding + tie-breaker
3) Allison/Sean review caching TTL + rate limits for SRE signoff

Chloe Martin: 👍 will post updated contract tomorrow.

Felix Schneider: thx. also pls add examples for:
- compare period (prev range)
- breakdown by (region,tier)
- error response for invalid range

Chloe Martin: noted.

[Next day]

Chloe Martin: updated doc w endpoints + examples: go/redwood/unit-econ-api-contract#v2
highlights:
- GET /timeseries (supports `compare=previous_period`)
- POST /breakdown (cursor pagination)
- `view=live|finance`, response includes `meta.watermark_ts`, `meta.partial`
- validation: max window + restricted group_by combos

Allison Grant: reviewed. caching policy looks ok. one tweak: for finance view, TTL 15m is fine but set `stale-if-error=24h` so we don’t flap exec review if warehouse hiccups.

Sean Gallagher: +1. also add explicit 503 vs 504 semantics? if warehouse timeout, return 503 with `meta.stale_served=true` when we serve stale cache.

Chloe Martin: cool. added `meta.stale_served` + `meta.cache_age_seconds`.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: RBAC section looks good. please ensure endpoints are under `/internal/...` and also gated by `X-Redwood-Internal: true` header like other internal console APIs.

Chloe Martin: yep.

Jada Williams: cursor encoding: please base64(json) not base64(“string|string”). easier to evolve. but we can still treat it as opaque.

Chloe Martin: changed to base64(JSON) with fields: `last_metric_value`, `last_group_key`. server treats unknown fields as ignore.

Mei Lin: 👍 from data side. when you implement, please default breakdown grain to `day` (hourly breakdown tables get big fast).

Nora Ahmed: works. UI will use day unless user toggles hour.

Dylan Brooks: looks ready for ENG-8427. ship it.

source.body:
Talia Benson: Kicking off the Private Day-2 backup/restore toolkit requirements thread. Need to land on (1) RPO/RTO targets we’ll *commit* to by archetype, (2) what state is in-scope vs out, (3) who owns customer-facing docs vs internal runbooks. Please reply w/ what you’re hearing from customers + any “must include” components.

Vivek Kulkarni: From customer calls last 2 weeks: they don’t care about “backup” as a feature; they care about “I can recover and pass audit.” RPO/RTO asks are all over the place but recurring:
- VPC single-region: RPO 1h–4h, RTO 2h–8h (depends on whether we’re restoring into fresh cluster vs in-place)
- On-prem: RPO 24h is acceptable for some, but they want deterministic restore steps and evidence.
Also: they expect backups encrypted w/ customer-managed keys where available.

Ethan Park: Component inventory (control plane) that I think needs to be explicitly covered:
- Config DB (tenant config, routing policies, model pins, quotas)
- Secrets *references* (NOT secret material) – we should restore pointers/IDs, not values, esp if using KMS/HSM
- Object store metadata (if any) re: model artifacts cached? but probably out-of-scope for Day-2 unless we’re storing customer artifacts
- K8s manifests/Helm values for Redwood services (needed for deterministic redeploy)
We should be clear: data plane is stateless and should be redeployable from desired state.

Sean Gallagher: +1 on clarity. SRE perspective: if we ship this, on-call will get paged when it fails. So we need to define:
- what “success” means (backup integrity + restorable)
- expected cadence (daily? hourly?)
- how to validate (smoke test + periodic restore drill in staging)
RTO targets must include “restore validation time,” not just “we ran a command.”

Neha Kapoor: Agree. We should treat this as reliability surface area. I’d like SLO-ish definitions:
- Backup job success rate over 30d
- Restore drill success rate + p95 restore drill duration
We don’t need to over-promise for GA, but we need to publish what we *support*.

Marcus Lin: Security constraints to bake in early:
- Backups encrypted at rest by default (envelope encryption). Customer-managed keys when possible; document fallback behavior if KMS down.
- No plaintext secrets in backup artifacts. At most, store secret *names/paths* and require re-seeding secrets as a step.
- Audit logs: need retention guidance; some customers will require exporting audit logs to their SIEM / immutable store. If we touch audit logs, we should coordinate w/ Kevin.
Also: please define threat model assumptions for on-prem/air-gapped.

Connor O'Brien: Packaging angle (on-prem): if air-gapped, we need a signed/offline bundle for whatever CLI/scripts we ship. Also version compatibility: backup format version + restore checks.
If we want this in the installer repo, we need:
- deterministic artifact layout
- checksum manifest
- signature verification step (CI + runtime)

Elliot Price: On docs ownership: propose
- Customer-facing: docs site (Elliot owner, Kira writer)
- Internal/SRE: Confluence runbooks (Sean/Kira)
But we need input from Engineering on the exact steps and “known footguns.”

Kira Thompson: Works for me. If we can agree on in-scope components + prerequisites, I’ll start a runbook outline this week.
Question: are we including Kubernetes-level backups (Velero) or explicitly *not* and only doing app-level snapshots?

Bruno Silva: From Helm/Terraform side: customers are already doing Velero/etc. I think we should support two layers:
1) Redwood control-plane state backup (DB snapshot + config export + manifest)
2) Optional: guidance for k8s resources (Velero include/exclude list)
But we should avoid “Velero required” as a hard dep.

Miles Thornton: DR architecture take: don’t conflate “cluster recovery” w/ “app restore.”
- For etcd snapshot restore: infra playbook. Use only for catastrophic cluster corruption.
- For “restore into clean cluster”: our primary supported story.
RTO should be measured for the supported story (clean cluster), not worst-case etcd surgery.

Hanae Suzuki: On KMS/HSM: some private cloud customers have KMS-like APIs but not AWS/GCP. If we do a provider interface, we can support at least:
- AWS KMS
- GCP KMS (maybe later)
- on-prem HSM via PKCS#11? (might be too heavy for v1)
We should document key rotation expectations and what happens if decrypt fails.

Talia Benson: Super helpful. Trying to converge on a first pass:
In-scope (v1):
- Control plane config DB snapshot + restore
- Export of install config/Helm values + required k8s manifests to deterministically redeploy
- Secrets: references only; explicit step to re-provision secrets (w/ validation checks)
- Backup artifact format + encryption + integrity verification
- Restore validation (smoke tests)
Out-of-scope (v1):
- Data plane token/KV cache state
- Full cluster recovery (etcd restore) beyond a pointer to infra runbook
- Customer application data

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: but “smoke tests” needs definition. Suggest minimum:
- Control plane API health
- tenant config read/write
- routing policy present
- ability to issue a no-op inference request (or at least hit model registry endpoint)
- audit log pipeline healthy (if enabled)

Ethan Park: For control plane state: we need to confirm what datastore it is per deployment. If Postgres: pg_dump/pg_basebackup? If something else, we need consistent snapshot semantics. I’ll write up what we actually run in VPC installs.

Marcus Lin: Add explicit note: backups must be encrypted *before* leaving node (no “upload plaintext then encrypt server-side”). If we support S3 SSE-KMS, still prefer client-side envelope encryption; SSE-KMS can be additional layer.

Connor O'Brien: +1. Also we need “restore is deterministic”: same inputs => same outputs, including versioned manifests. If restore detects mismatch (backup from newer version), it should fail loud with guidance.

Elliot Price: On customer-facing doc: we can frame as “supported DR patterns” + checklists. But we need a clear ownership contact for escalations (Solutions/SRE). Also: disclaimers about what support can assist with vs customer responsibilities (e.g., secrets re-seeding).

Vivek Kulkarni: Customer asks to capture in doc:
- “Where are my backups stored?” (S3/NFS/etc)
- “How do I test restores without taking prod down?” (restore into staging / parallel cluster)
- “How do I prove compliance?” (evidence: logs, checksum manifests, last successful restore drill)
FinBank specifically asked for RPO<=1h on control-plane config; MedData more focused on retention/audit evidence.

Talia Benson: Action items:
- Ethan: draft “what state exists + snapshot mechanism” for control plane by end of week
- Sean/Neha: propose initial RPO/RTO targets + restore drill cadence we can support
- Marcus/Hanae: security requirements bullet list (encryption, secrets, audit logs)
- Connor: packaging/signing checklist stub
- Elliot/Kira: draft doc/runbook outline (customer vs internal)
I’ll compile into a spec draft and share in #eng + link in Linear.

Sean Gallagher: Works. Also can we avoid calling this “backup” in the UI? Customers will assume it covers everything. Maybe “control plane state backup” + clear scope callout.

Ethan Park: +1. Naming matters.

Marcus Lin: +1. Scope creep is the #1 risk here.

Talia Benson: Noted. I’ll use “Private control-plane backup/restore” in the spec.

(reactji) Neha Kapoor reacted with :thumbsup:
(reactji) Connor O'Brien reacted with :white_check_mark:
(reactji) Elliot Price reacted with :memo:

source.messages:
Sahana Iyer: trying to lock down an integration test plan for split accuracy (route experiments) before we ask for any kind of load test. what's the best way to validate allocation % + determinism without spinning up a giant traffic generator?

Leila Farouk: +1. e2e tests today can prove “request gets variant header” but not “50/50 is actually 50/50”. we also want something that runs in CI and doesn’t flap.

Jordan Lee: we can get most of the confidence with simulation (no GPUs, no real traffic). assignment is deterministic hash -> bucket -> variant. so test the assigner as a pure function.

Jordan Lee: proposal:
1) generate N synthetic stickiness keys (uuid4 / random 128-bit) with a fixed seed.
2) run `assign(experiment_config, key)` for each key.
3) count variant hits, assert within tolerance for expected split.
4) repeat for a few configs (50/50, 90/10, 34/33/33, tiny buckets) and for “missing key” paths.

Jordan Lee: importantly: treat it as a statistical test w/ deterministic input. don’t rely on true randomness.

Leila Farouk: what N are we thinking? and what tolerance so it’s not flaky?

Jordan Lee: N=100k is cheap in unit/integration land (just hashing). if we don’t want that big, N=20k might be ok. tolerance can be based on binomial stddev.

Jordan Lee: e.g. expected p=0.5, N=20k => sigma ~ sqrt(N*p*(1-p)) ~ 70.7. 5 sigma is 353, so assert counts in [9650, 10350] (±1.75%). that’s extremely unlikely to fail given fixed seed anyway.

Jada Williams: +1 on simulation. we should also test “precedence + determinism across restarts” (same config version, same key => same variant).

Jada Williams: another thing: for multi-variant, use chi-square instead of per-variant tolerance. but per-variant bounds are easier to understand.

Vikram Singh: this covers the bucketing lib, but we also need router integration: ensure we’re not accidentally re-hashing different keys depending on headers present.

Vikram Singh: can do a lightweight integration test against router/policy engine in-process:
- stand up policy evaluator w/ an experiment config
- feed request contexts with different combinations of stickiness inputs (customer_id, user_id header, session_id, fallback)
- ensure key precedence matches ADR and assigned variant matches the pure assigner for the resolved key

Logan Wright: + reminder: if folks try to use metrics to validate split accuracy, it’s noisy in staging due to sampling + attribution drop. for correctness tests, prefer the pure assignment + router debug endpoint output.

Caleb Johnson: +1. telemetry is great for prod monitoring, not for CI validation of distribution.

Leila Farouk: can we put this under “integration tests” even if it’s mostly simulation? i’m thinking we keep it close to ENG-9031 scope.

Jordan Lee: yep. we can call it “distribution simulation test” but run it in the same suite.

Sahana Iyer: does anyone have a suggested structure for the test so it doesn’t take forever in CI?

Jordan Lee: rough pseudocode:

```python
import random
from collections import Counter

SEED = 1337
N = 20000

configs = [
  ("50-50", {"A": 0.5, "B": 0.5}),
  ("90-10", {"A": 0.9, "B": 0.1}),
  ("3-way", {"A": 0.34, "B": 0.33, "C": 0.33}),
]

def gen_keys(n):
  r = random.Random(SEED)
  return [f"user-{r.getrandbits(128):032x}" for _ in range(n)]

keys = gen_keys(N)

for name, split in configs:
  counts = Counter(assign(split, k) for k in keys)
  for v, p in split.items():
    # 5-sigma bound + small epsilon
    mu = N * p
    sigma = (N * p * (1 - p)) ** 0.5
    assert abs(counts[v] - mu) <= max(5*sigma, 0.005*N)
```

Jordan Lee: since keys are deterministic, it’s not flaky. runtime is basically N hash ops per config.

Rafael Mendes: can we also add a skew regression test for “missing stickiness key” defaulting? we had that bug where it used low-entropy / same value => variant 0 traffic.

Jordan Lee: yes. for missing key behavior we should test:
- if stickiness key missing and we fall back to IP hash, distribution should still look ok across synthetic IPs
- if we fall back to a constant or too-low entropy value, it’ll show up as >99% in one bucket. we can assert that “resolved key must not be constant” in validation, but also include a test that simulates “missing key” path produces non-degenerate distribution when given varying fallbacks.

Vikram Singh: router-side we can call the internal assignment debug endpoint (in test mode) and validate:
- it reports which key source it used (customer_id vs header vs session)
- it reports config version
- it returns variant_id
then compare that variant_id to the pure assigner output.

Leila Farouk: that’s nice because it tests the “resolved key” logic without spinning up full tracing/metrics.

Sahana Iyer: ok, so plan is:
1) simulation distribution test for bucketer
2) in-process router/policy evaluator test that compares debug output vs pure assigner
3) a tiny smoke e2e just to ensure headers propagate (variant header present) (already have)

Jada Williams: yep. and maybe one more: “config change doesn’t remap users unless split boundaries change” (sticky). e.g. if you rename a variant but keep same allocation, are we stable? depends on variant_id hashing input.

Jordan Lee: good call. in ADR we said hashing uses stable `variant_id` ordering + cumulative split. so if variant IDs change, assignment changes. we should make that explicit in tests + docs. for tests, assert that reordering variants in config does NOT change mapping (we normalize by variant_id sort) but changing variant_id does.

Caleb Johnson: please also keep N modest. 20k * 3 configs is fine. 100k might be ok but python overhead could be higher in CI.

Sahana Iyer: got it. i’ll start with N=20k, 3 configs, 5-sigma bound + 0.5% floor.

Redwood CI Bot: buildkite: started job `eng/integration-tests (route-experiments)` on branch `sahana/split-sim-tests`

Leila Farouk: can you add a note in the test comment about why we use sigma bounds so future folks don’t “tighten” it and cause pain?

Sahana Iyer: yep.

Rafael Mendes: also add a quick assertion for “no variant gets <1% traffic when configured >10%” (catches totally broken paths). sigma bounds should already catch it but the explicit guard reads well.

Jordan Lee: +1. and we should pin the seed so any failure is reproducible locally.

Sahana Iyer: cool. i’ll write up the test plan in ENG-9031 and link this thread. thanks all.

Leila Farouk: :thumbsup:

Vikram Singh: :+1: ping me when you have the debug-endpoint compare test, i want to make sure it matches router behavior.

Logan Wright: and if anyone wants to cross-check in staging later, use the experiment health dashboard, but don’t block CI on telemetry numbers.

Sahana Iyer: acknowledged.

source.content:
Dylan Brooks: Quick q before we merge more of the dashboard work: who *owns* the experiment results API? (the thing Console calls to get variant deltas). I’m seeing Console backend, telemetry, and platform all touching it and I want a clean boundary.

Chloe Martin: +1, we need to be explicit. Today I have a WIP endpoint in console-api that does a query + computes deltas. But if telemetry wants to expose a service we should align now.

Logan Wright: Telemetry POV: we own attribution schema + rollups, but I don’t want telemetry on the hook for a customer-facing API surface unless we sign up for authz/rbac, pagination, etc.

Jada Williams: Platform POV: experiment-service owns config/state machine, not analytics. Policy engine assigns {experiment_id, variant_id} at request time. Anything “results” feels downstream of telemetry.

Dylan Brooks: Right. My concern is: if Console backend owns API but results are wrong / stale due to rollups, where does it land? Support is going to ask “who do I page?”.

Mei Lin: Analytics/warehouse perspective: we can provide a canonical dataset + contract (tables, freshness SLO, definitions for p50/p95/p99/error/cost) and data quality checks. But API should probably live with Console so it can enforce RBAC and present consistent responses.

Caleb Johnson: +1. Also we’ll likely iterate schema/cardinality safeguards. Having an API in telemetry that then has to coordinate deploys w/ Console sounds painful.

Ethan Park: Agree on separation: control plane gives you experiment definitions and current status; data plane emits attribution; telemetry aggregates; Console renders + exposes customer-facing endpoints.

Chloe Martin: So proposal:
1) Console backend owns `/experiments/{id}/results` (customer-facing) + response shape + RBAC.
2) Telemetry/Analytics owns the underlying rollup tables + freshness + definitions.
3) Platform owns config/state + debug assignment endpoint.

Dylan Brooks: That sounds right. But for “results computation” specifically: do we compute deltas in Console backend or in analytics job?

Mei Lin: Prefer deltas computed in the query layer (Console backend) *based on pre-aggregated metrics*. We can store both absolute metrics per {route, experiment_id, variant_id, window} and let Console compute “B - A” so UX can change without backfilling.

Logan Wright: Caution: computing p99 deltas from rollups is fine, but anything that needs joins across raw spans is a no-go in Console API. We should codify “Console API only queries rollup_* tables; no raw OTel reads.”

Chloe Martin: Works for me. I’ll implement results API as:
- read experiment config/status from experiment-service (to validate variants + route scope)
- query analytics rollups keyed by {experiment_id, variant_id, route_id, region?}
- compute deltas + guardrail status
- include metadata about freshness (last_aggregated_ts)

Jordan Lee: One nuance: precedence rules (experiment vs canary/fallback). For results, we should trust the attribution emitted after routing decision. Don’t try to “re-derive” which model got picked in Console.

Jada Williams: +1. Results should be based on emitted `variant_id` not recomputing assignment. Otherwise you’ll get weirdness when fallback triggers.

Dylan Brooks: Ok. What about on-call / pager? If results are stale vs wrong vs API down.

Logan Wright: Let’s split operational ownership:
- API availability/errors: Console on-call (Chloe’s team)
- rollup freshness / attribution drop rate / cardinality issues: Telemetry on-call (me/Caleb)
- config propagation / assignment correctness: Platform on-call (Ethan/Jordan)

Chloe Martin: I can add “source of truth” fields in the results response for debugging:
- `rollup_window_minutes`
- `data_freshness_lag_seconds`
- `attribution_drop_rate` (if we have it)
Not sure we want to expose all of that to customers, but at least internal.

Mei Lin: We can add internal-only fields behind the `X-Redwood-Debug: 1` header or an internal route. Otherwise customers will over-index on it.

Dylan Brooks: Makes sense.

Ethan Park: Also: any customer-facing results endpoint should not call experiment-service for every request if we can avoid it. Cache experiment metadata in Console API with a short TTL. Failure mode should be “serve results w/ stale config” not “results endpoint hard down.”

Chloe Martin: Yup. I’ll do a 60s TTL cache; if experiment-service is unavailable we’ll still return rollups but mark config as stale.

Logan Wright: Re: definitions: telemetry will publish a short “metrics contract” doc so we’re all aligned on p95/p99 semantics, cost attribution (prompt vs completion tokens), and error rate denominator (requests vs attempts).

Dylan Brooks: Great. Action items:
- Chloe: add a note in PR description + code comments: Console owns results API surface; queries rollups only.
- Logan/Mei: add metrics contract + freshness expectations + how to interpret missing data.
- Ethan/Jordan/Jada: confirm which service owns the “experiment status” state machine (running/degraded/stopped) and what Console should display when guardrails trip.

Chloe Martin: Ack. I’ll also drop a quick RACI in the PRD / eng notes so this doesn’t come up again.

Caleb Johnson: :thumbsup: also please tag telemetry early if you need new dimensions; we’re watching cardinality like a hawk.

Dylan Brooks: :+1: thanks all. I’ll reference this thread in ENG-9027/PR-28644 so release coordination knows who to chase.
